tasks.name: N3: Mem controller verification
tasks.description:
###############################################################################
# Mem controller verification (03-11-2025)
## **Link to important documentation:**
Test Plan: [Mem\_controller\_Test\_Plan.xlsx](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:x:/g/personal/sharanya_shetty_ixana_ai/ET1uzK7BvlFEqR7-iJJO_VgBmtiJKqO4rJd0YMxrbJz7fQ?e=MuF03C)
PPT: [config\_mem\_controller.pptx](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/g/personal/sharanya_shetty_ixana_ai/ESktLlhTOUlMlT6qi3bAttsBbVtFH2tPPX4cEu1t8fm6Lw?e=yiM2u0)
## Contents of a Mem controller Verification PPT: [config\_mem\_controller.pptx](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/g/personal/sharanya_shetty_ixana_ai/ESktLlhTOUlMlT6qi3bAttsBbVtFH2tPPX4cEu1t8fm6Lw?e=yiM2u0)
1. Waveform verification
2. Code coverage
## **How to run?**

**Sanity verification:**
1. go to: /home/SharanyaShetty/digital/qs223/IC-RTL-qs223/vcs/config\_mem\_controller/verification
2. put the command: ./compile.rtl\_sim and ./simv\_rtl –gui &

**Code coverage:**
1. go to: /home/SharanyaShetty/digital/qs223/IC-RTL-qs223/vcs/config\_mem\_controller/code\_coverage
2. put the command: ./compile.rtl\_sim and verdi -cov -covdir simv\_rtl.vdb
## Timeline
**Task Progress Summary** [@Archisman Ghosh](#user_mention#96272410)
**03/11/2025** – Debugged the testbench provided by [@Souptik Dolui](#user_mention#89230203) for regression and code coverage
\[**Review scheduled for 04/11** due to [@Archisman Ghosh](#user_mention#96272410) unavailability on 03/11/2025\]

**Non-technical summary** [@Pushpal Das](#user_mention#89199382)
Wrapped up the task on 03/10/2025 (started on 03/10/2025); review is scheduled on 04/10/2025.

comments.text: @Pushpal Das : I handed off this task to @Sharanya Shetty on 31st October with the Testbench complete. Then after she ran the tests and meeting was conducted with @Archisman Ghosh
comments.author: Souptik Dolui

comments.text: @Pushpal Das : Wrapped up the task on 03/10/2025 (started on 03/10/2025); review is scheduled on 04/10/2025. this delay was not on my end, I was assigned this later! @Archisman Ghosh can you confirm this
comments.author: Sharanya Shetty

comments.text: Mem Controller Testbench has been created and KT regarding the same has been provided to @Sharanya Shetty. She will take it up from here
comments.author: Souptik Dolui

comments.text: I will start making the APB read write tests for this verification from tomorrow
comments.author: Souptik Dolui

tasks.name: Rerunning PS+PHY+SPI_FIFO+Configmem test with 2 devices
tasks.description:
Deliverable:
Bit file address: C:\\Users\\ixana\\Documents\\Bitstreams
*   V4\_both\_dev\_working.bit is the singular bitstream used for the tests here: QS222 FPGA Test Plan ([https://app.clickup.com/9017196379/docs/8cqezuv-45897/8cqezuv-45797](https://app.clickup.com/9017196379/docs/8cqezuv-45897/8cqezuv-45797))
## Instructions
### VIVADO
1. Open Visual Studio Code:
    1. Log into the FPGA computer or via remote access using Chrome Remote Desktop
        1. Password: 104747
2. In the start menu, launch Vivado 2023.2
    1. Note: It will take a few seconds to open the application GUI
3. In the **Tasks** section, click **Open Hardware Manager**
4. Ensure the FPGA is turned on and USB hub is connected to test PC
5. Click **Open target > Auto Connect**
6. Once the device is detected, click **Program device**
7. Select the three dots next to the bitstream file section
8. Open V4\_both\_dev\_working.bit and click OK
9. Ignore the Debug probe message
10. Click **Program** to flash the bitstream to the FPGA
### PYTHON
1. Open Visual Studio Code:
    1. Log into the FPGA computer or via remote access using Chrome Remote Desktop
        1. Password: 104747
2. Click File > Open Folder... > Documents > Select Folder
3. Open a local terminal, i.e. Teminal > New Terminal
4. Start a virtual environment in the powershell terminal:
    1. Navigate to C:\\Users\\ixana\\Documents\\Aneesh\\IC-testbench
    2. Run `Set-ExecutionPolicy -Scope Process -ExecutionPolicy Bypass` to allow all scripts to run in powershell without restrictions
5. Run `.\.venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1` to start a virtual environment (i.e. `(.venv)`)
6. Now from the IC-testbench repo, navigate to IC-testbench\\ic\\QS222\\post\_tapeout\_fpga\_scripts
7. Begin an interactive test using `python .\qs222_test_enhanced.py -i` and follow the prompts in the terminal
#### SPI\_FIFO
1. initial > fifo
    1. ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/a83b87ac-9cb0-4e1c-9820-91c48ab722d5/image.png)
2. PS
#### PER tests
1. qam2\_dr0
    1. ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/84066dd1-57c6-4f69-83a2-7219c080adb0/image.png)

#### Configmem
1. initial > efuse
    1. ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/575870c5-5b04-4507-a35a-78520673370d/image.png)

tasks.name: N3: Create new models from 223 and push to github
tasks.description:
**Why:** Ixana plans to expedite pre silicon validation.
**Deliverable:** a) github link with recreatable testbench. b) PPT with list of models c) Make sure it is FPGA friendly, each module should go through quick validation d) Any feedbacks if there. e) A separate meeting should be scheduled and recorded. f) Presentation of all the analog modules and their connections.
 Not no lint error should be present.
**Risks (T,E):**
(T)
(E) Some of the feedback might need time
**Limitations** (Assumptions (A), Constraints (C), Dependency (D))
(A) NA
(C) i) DVCO- This model cannot be made purely FPGA-friendly because there are 2^13 possibilities of clock output and we cannot get these outputs using simple clock dividers.
ii) The above model can provide clocks up to ~13GHz. But Zync Ultrascale+ can give i/o operations up to 7.125 GHz.
(D) NA
**Results** (to be updated after task is complete):
*   Github links:
    1. **RTL Filelist:** [https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/vcs/models/script/rtl.flist\_dvco](https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/vcs/models/script/rtl.flist_dvco)
    2. **Testbench:** [https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/test/models/vco\_tb.sv](https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/test/models/vco_tb.sv)
*   PPT:
    *   [https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/r/personal/abhishek\_sarkar\_ixana\_ai/\_layouts/15/Doc2.aspx?action=edit&sourcedoc=%7B0f12d826-be25-407d-9783-163ab3bd0835%7D&wdOrigin=TEAMS-MAGLEV.teamsSdk\_ns.rwc&wdExp=TEAMS-TREATMENT&wdhostclicktime=1755674953898&web=1](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/r/personal/abhishek_sarkar_ixana_ai/_layouts/15/Doc2.aspx?action=edit&sourcedoc=%7B0f12d826-be25-407d-9783-163ab3bd0835%7D&wdOrigin=TEAMS-MAGLEV.teamsSdk_ns.rwc&wdExp=TEAMS-TREATMENT&wdhostclicktime=1755674953898&web=1)
    *   Slides 2-7

tasks.name: N3: PHY changes in RTL
tasks.description:
**Why:** PHY changes are required to accommodate encryption.
**Deliverable:** a) RTL changes and initial verification with code cleanup. b) Code review for a. Meeting recording should be shared. c) RTL changes for PHY + AES. Should restore previous functionality. Should support OOK path too. d) Code review for c. e) Lint/CDC PPT f) Basic functionality check after e. g) Constraint file and PD related files.
**Risks (T,E):**
(T) NA
(E)
**Limitations** (Assumptions (A), Constraints (C), Dependency (D))
(A) NA
(C) NA
(D) Keying might be changed.

tasks.name: N3: Timing Diagram & Block interactions for DCO calibration logic
tasks.description:
Why: Need a formalized way to present all the interactions & timings for the calibration loop for smoother RTL design flow
 Deliverable : 
1) Timing diagrams of major modules
2) Interactions between all major and minor modules present in the loop

Risk
1) Although logic is clear, creating an accurate python model for timing might take longer than a day
2) Python model/timing diagram might not accurately predict timing issues in actual RTL

Limitations
1) Final bit widths needs from AC, before delivery to digital team

comments.text: Roy was not well and Archisman needed timing diagram and that was not budgeted in the task.

tasks.name: QS223 CUSTOM LAYOUT

comments.text:
Kirit: Delay reason 
1) While designing the UWB filter, we had to do a couple of iterations to meet the bandwidth requirements. 
2) We changed the LPF layout a few times to reduce parasitics onthe  LPF o/p node. We had to bring RSSI and AGC i/p close to the LPF output to reduce the parasitic cap. 
3) UWB Clock buffer: We changed the clock buffer layout a few times to meet the delay requirements. 
4) We had to modify the integrator to meet the CDR PLL 320MHz requirements 
5) There was a change in BR_mixer and BR LPF design 
6) Added source follower at the output of ED and LPF 
7) Changed floor plan many times to meet the top-level routing requirements and optimize the critical signal path. (We brought BE_top closer to the ED o/p and SS_top closer to the LPF/ED o/p. 
8) There was a change in the Spectrum sensor design.
comments.author: KiritkumarP

tasks.name: N3: Running VNC simulation for core tests with error introduction in both RTL and FPGA setup: 18th Feb to 4th March
tasks.description:
**Core Schedule Tests With Error Introduction In ASIC RTL Setup:**
RTL Flist to run core schedule tests with error introduction in ASIC RTL setup: [https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/vcs/qs223\_top\_cleaned/script/rtl.flist\_core\_error\_test](https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/vcs/qs223_top_cleaned/script/rtl.flist_core_error_test)
Compile Script to run core schedule tests with error introduction in ASIC RTL Setup: [https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/vcs/qs223\_top\_cleaned/script/compile.rtl\_sim\_core\_error\_test](https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/vcs/qs223_top_cleaned/script/compile.rtl_sim_core_error_test)

Command to Run Simulation for Core Test with Error in ASIC RTL Setup:
First navigate to main branch in IC-RTL-qs223 repo. There, go to vcs/qs223\_top\_cleaned folder.
Here, type the following command in terminal to compile:

```python
./script/compile.rtl_sim_core_error_test
```

Type the following command in terminal to open the simulation GUI in Verdi:

```plain
./simv_rtl -gui &
```

**Core Schedule Tests With Error Introduction In FPGA Setup:**
RTL Flist to run core schedule tests with error introduction in FPGA setup: [https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/qs223\_fpga\_sumon/vcs/models\_fpga/script\_v2/rtl.flist\_qs223\_top\_two\_dev](https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/qs223_fpga_sumon/vcs/models_fpga/script_v2/rtl.flist_qs223_top_two_dev)
Compile Script to run core schedule tests with error introduction in FPGA setup: [https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/qs223\_fpga\_sumon/vcs/models\_fpga/script\_v2/compile.rtl\_two\_dev](https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/qs223_fpga_sumon/vcs/models_fpga/script_v2/compile.rtl_two_dev)

Command to Run Simulation for Core Test with Error in FPGA Setup:
First navigate to qs223\_fpga\_sumon branch. There, go to vcs/models\_fpga folder.
Here type the following command in terminal to compile:

```python
./script_v2/compile.rtl_two_dev
```

Type the following command in terminal to open the simulation GUI in Verdi:

```plain
./simv_rtl_two_dev -gui &
```

tasks.name: N3: Running VNC simulation with core+wir using updated QS223 netlist for FPGA:

comments.text:
@Archisman Ghosh I was running VNC simulations for core+wir using updated QS223 netlist from 6th Feb to 27th Feb. There were multiple iterations for this task. We were modifying the RTL for FPGA version to fix synthesis and implementation issues in Vivado, and I ran VNC simulation every time some changes were made in modules for FPGA: Ran baseband video demo test, rf video demo test in core bypass mode, ran core schedule test (basic schedule, complex schedule, all slots schedule test) and core fifo readback test.

After this, I ran VNC sims for core tests with error introduction which continued upto 11th March. Since there is a separate task created for that, so I have mentioned the end date of this task as 27th Feb and end date of error tests with core as 11th March
comments.author: Ushasi Das

tasks.name: N3: JPEG XL based video codec study example and spec out (Initial version)
tasks.description:
**Why:** Ixana plans to integrate Video Codec for its display.
**Deliverable:** a) A Python code with no build in function. This will help to understand all the blocks and memory requirement and take further call. b) A PPT/clickup with possible block diagram/architecture. image could be AI-generated or handdrawn in time crunch, but interfaces/configs and memory requirements should be clear with arithmetic calculation. c) Task is completed Archisman Shovan receives b and able to run a from repo d) In case of blocker, a clear ppt explaining so.
Example PPT: [https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/g/personal/archisman\_ghosh\_ixana\_ai/EUwRMyZGkz9Ek8\_gp2bmXYwB3jDFmgFaQPPPFq0734w\_eg?e=AWDbJ9](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/g/personal/archisman_ghosh_ixana_ai/EUwRMyZGkz9Ek8_gp2bmXYwB3jDFmgFaQPPPFq0734w_eg?e=AWDbJ9)
Example ipynb: [https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/python\_tb/JPEG\_RnD/JPEG\_compression.ipynb](https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/python_tb/JPEG_RnD/JPEG_compression.ipynb)
1. Note this is written for JPEG.
2. [@Ushasi Das](#user_mention#89319673) can help you coding a few steps.
3. Every step in the PPT should be a different cell.
4. No cell should have a python library used. it will always be the way hardware can implement. Its goal is to suggest hardware designers to optimize.
**Risks (T,E):**
(T) Availability of good research paper. understanding some documents might have unprecedented delay.
(T) Not a well-defined algorithm defined in the white paper for variable block division required for varDCT mode (lossy compression)
(E) Few steps of the coding algorithm are highly hardware intensive, requiring lots of arithmetic blocks and parallel storage
**Limitations** (Assumptions (A), Constraints (C), Dependency (D))
(A) Codec will be used for real-time video transfer, so a few features of JPEG-XL might not be used. Eg. Progressive decoding
(C) AG will have limited access to laptop/Internet July 4 weekend. So this task should be finished by July 2
(D)
**Results** (to be updated after task is complete) \[Added by AS after ppt presentation on 31 Jun\]:
a. Python repo link:
1. Lossy Compression with VarDCT:

[

github.com

https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/python\_tb/JPEG\_RnD/JPEGXL\_VarDCT\_Abhishek.ipynb

](https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/python_tb/JPEG_RnD/JPEGXL_VarDCT_Abhishek.ipynb)

1. Lossless Compression in Modular Mode:

[

github.com

https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/python\_tb/JPEG\_RnD/JPEGXL\_Modular\_mode\_Ushasi.ipynb

](https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223/blob/main/python_tb/JPEG_RnD/JPEGXL_Modular_mode_Ushasi.ipynb)

b. PPT/doc link:  [https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/r/personal/ushasi\_das\_ixana\_ai/\_layouts/15/doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7Bbf2773c5-be9c-419c-84e5-b9e87af0fa83%7D&action=edit](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/r/personal/ushasi_das_ixana_ai/_layouts/15/doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7Bbf2773c5-be9c-419c-84e5-b9e87af0fa83%7D&action=edit)
(T)iger is a risk that everybody understands
(E)lephant is a risk that I know but others don't think so, yet

**AG comments 30 Jun**:
This task will be deprioritized. SS may work on AES verification

**UD Comments 23rd July:**
I have completed the coding for JPEG XL Modular mode (lossless mode) and I am getting perfectly reconstructed image. I have attached the links below.

tasks.name: N3: GUI based preset + config generation recreate + demo
tasks.description:
# N3: GUI based preset + config generation recreate + demo (02-01-2026)
* * *
## Modified Files
### Build Scripts
*   `ic/common/config_convert.py` - Added CLI argument support
*   `ic/common/config_to_py.py` - Added CLI argument support
*   `ic/common/presets_validator.py` - Smart sampling, int=1, jump by number
### QS223 Module Files
*   `ic/qs223/__init__.py` - Fixed imports and chip registration
*   `ic/qs223/QS223_presets.py` - Fixed import paths
*   `ic/qs223/register_defaults.py` - Fixed import paths
*   `ic/qs223/post_process_calculation.py` - Fixed import paths
*   `ic/qs223/specific_dataclasses.py` - Fixed import paths
*   `ic/qs223/ic_regs.py` - Added re-export compatibility
* * *
## Created Files
### QS223 Setup
*   `ic/qs223/config.xlsx` - Chip configuration
*   `ic/qs223/config_new.csv` - Cleaned configuration
*   `ic/qs223/qs223_ic_regs.py` - Generated register classes
### Documentation
*   `QS223_SETUP_README_CLEAN.md` - Setup documentation
* * *
## Timeline
**02/01/2026:** QS223 GUI based preset, and documentation complete
\[Vacation\]
**12/01/2026:** QS223 GUI setup completed
**13/01/2026: QS223 DEMO shared**

## Prerequisites
*   Python 3.12, VS Code, Git, required permissions
## GUI Operations 
*   Create/open QS223 configs (registers, test, memfile folders)
*   Define register settings with presets
*   Check diff for debugging
*   Export raw bits to memfile
GUI demo: [GUI DEMO-20260113\_113359-Meeting Recording.mp4](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/sharanya_shetty_ixana_ai/IQAyohGs_w7RQLUyBYLX6FqKAazzceY_UvsuYU2ancfNfok?nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJPbmVEcml2ZUZvckJ1c2luZXNzIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXciLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJNeUZpbGVzTGlua0NvcHkifX0&e=WTPS2d)

comments.text:
@Archisman Ghosh @Pushpal Das I have completed the setup for combined Phy+QSPI+FIFO with PS, config mem controller and all config mems. I did the synthesis and implementation and I was able to generate bitstream for this. There were some RTL changes in QSPI+Phy+FIFO block so I had to repeat synthesis so this took few iterations.
Currently, the behavioral simulation and post-synthesis simulation in Vivado is not working as expected. I am debugging these with the help of @Souptik Dolui. This debugging is causing some more delay.

AFE Integration is Completed and Reviewed on Monday(10/Nov/2025)
Can @Archisman Ghosh close the task
comments.author: Manash Dey

comments.text:
Summary: In the Receiver AFE Integration, we have integrated the Modules- 3Comparators, Clock Recovery Modules, Clock Recovery Calibration Module, Retimer, Demapper. 
A test Bench is made where Transmitter Output is going to the Input of the Receiver and We are Checking whether the Output of Rx is similar with the Tx Input. Yes, it's coming similar with first 60ns of Margin. We have also designed Retimer in this Time Span.
comments.author: Manash Dey

comments.text: When did you start this task?
comments.author: Archisman Ghosh

comments.text: Please change the End Date to 10Nov
comments.author: Manash Dey

comments.text:
In AFE Integration, We have to go for the half rate Data
That's why we had to change our architecture for sampling the present State & previous State and Also I've to change the Decoding Logic to get the 3 Outputs of the Receiver
Moreover, Buffer designing may get some more time
comments.author: Manash Dey

tasks.name: N3: ARM core ready and subsystem implementation for verification
tasks.description:
Deliverable:
a) Presentation with Subsystem:
b) Github link with code base:
c) regular updates
**AG 21 July Update:**
Installed arm compiler locally.
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/a01511df-ec7e-44d2-b2b8-d4d423fc741d/image.png)
We can generate in verilog hex file format
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/269645b8-e634-44f4-b9c0-b931330052a3/image.png)
Responded ARM with email chain.
Example linker file:
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/a37640d6-06f4-4987-8f41-6e45f9850c37/image.png)
Example C code:
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/0ded493d-d92a-41ff-b840-b69f45de5b3d/image.png)
generated OPCODE (asm):
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/f1e68fea-bcdb-44ed-86e9-c7a255eb6593/image.png)
**22 July update:**
We need to call armcc compiler from makefile to generate bin file.
./RunIK should be called to start the test.
Details to be explored.
ARM M0 architecture video finished.
**24 July update:**
Need to install it.
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/b7995617-4a37-4b08-8349-25a540b31e47/image.png)
**1st August Update: (update summary for last 1 week)**
M0/m0+ supports armcc which is not supported anymore. Able to checkout license using code-based licensing following the the email chain.
armclang compiler works now:
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/b06c7f6f-9eb9-43f8-b658-e1ea2b07c12b/image.png)
But still facing some issue which I have communicated.
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/39372858-1cac-494d-8cb7-ec492b517f1d/image.png)
To invoke compiler: ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/9af90423-9fc0-401c-bbd4-6fe6b1e95344/image.png)
**4th August Update:**
Neither of armcc/gcc compiler works. I have shared this with their support team.
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/f26d6997-94b0-47fa-93a8-eac85fef7585/image.png)
For armcc compiler:
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/93c65914-fcd5-47b5-b5fe-03281f76dd38/image.png)
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/00222db0-4d4b-42ad-8f96-11559202f18a/image.png)
**5th August Update:**
Able to fresh-copy and use the fresh patch.
Able to generate bin file using armclang compiler.
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/f9082ed5-9534-4955-babd-0a470d87ae1a/image.png)
\*This screenshot is just teh proof and a=path link. Need to run for full output.
Still not able to run the simulation, checking in the errors.
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/7b1c8fd0-f27f-4435-ac91-d073381ab097/image.png)
About GNU compiler, they suggested to use 4.8 version. Note that, we are in v13.
**6th August Update:**
Question to ARM and update:
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/88c35780-fa68-4075-9462-eb7324390e70/image.png)
Another update:
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/7052c302-3c74-469b-a9af-2aa68d843568/image.png)
**14th August Update:**
1.  Synopsys VCS 2024 is the simulation tool we use. However, ARM scripts don't work at this version which even they have confirmed. 
2. They told that ARM IP was verified with 2008 version, after that they cannot take responsibility but we need to understand and modify. 
3. They confirm that 2021, 2023 and 2025 version works. Our OS does not support 2021 or 2025. 
4. We are trying with 2023 but no luck. One suspicion is here we are using .bin file directly which is a system file not readable plaintext file. Now that has something to do with OS too. I have ARM compiler in my local laptop with Ubuntu but we use redhat to generate bin file and I am transfering then. Anyway they are not same. So next step will be to shift ARM compiler to server. I will need help from Antony in that. Shovan Maity is in CC there. 
5. Parallelly I started to go through the code to understand and change. I am using Ushasi's bandwidth there. I myself spending some active time as well. 
**15th August Update:**
1. I was parallelly trying to use our script to compile. Good news is I am able to compile after commenting tarmac file. However, I am trying to check and make sense of the simulation. It will need partial understanding (if not full) of ARM instruction set. I will go through the doc. This will be one of primary task of next week. 
2. Also, I receive some help from ARM to generate hex file which is readable from .bin file. I will try to create testbench accordingly. 
3. Note that 1 will give me more confidence when 5 of previous status is completed. 
4. I am still talking to Synopsys and ARM to fix the tool flow issue. 
**18th August Update:**
2023.12 has limited error but still not running.
Antony to install and try 2018.09 tomorrow.
Antony to request X-2025.06 as well.
**25th August Update:**
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/32e02027-a5c4-4d18-aed1-26f2ced4f9a2/image.png)
Able to run basic test.
Flow is manual at this point.
Local computer:
Change code in hellow.c
make hellow
Transfer hellow.bin to linux server
Server:
Transfer hellow.bin to image.bin in verification\_vcs
./compile.rtl\_sim
./simv\_rtl

**26Sept:**
Subsystem is integrated
3 UART, DMA, RAM, ROM models, Boot ROM, SSP, 6 timers are there. Diagram to be updated Monday.
**3rd October:**
![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/56ff4241-55d9-42da-8f92-885a47979bbd/image.png)
Final architecture.
Change points are captured at M0 core guide for FW ([https://app.clickup.com/9017196379/docs/8cqezuv-46917/8cqezuv-69577](https://app.clickup.com/9017196379/docs/8cqezuv-46917/8cqezuv-69577))

comments.text: @Shovan Maity closing the task. It has details from compiler to basic architecture setup/bringup. Architecture modification for our chip will be part of separate task and added based on the document link here.
comments.author: Archisman Ghosh

comments.text: Extended the task as all the interfaces are part of it now. DMA controller has been added too as requirement. Separate interfaces have been added which was discussed later. We may decide it to create separate task. Need to discuss with @Shovan Maity
comments.author: Archisman Ghosh

comments.text: @Shovan Maity it will take 2-3 weeks more as discussed. Once, we discuss, we may increase the deadline here.
comments.author: Archisman Ghosh

comments.text: We have lost almost 1 month with ARM communication which I estimated to be 1 weeks. Interface will probably take 3 more weeks.
comments.author: Archisman Ghosh

comments.text: Code base is ready, subsystem needs more information. I am able to compile and run a test which was the main responsibility of this task. Will discuss about @Shovan Maity about the task.
comments.author: Archisman Ghosh

comments.text: We are currently blocked by tool installation issue. This might cause potential delay. CC: @Shovan Maity
comments.author: Archisman Ghosh

31/07/2025
**A: Test Plan:** [https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:x:/p/shovan/EU8nHDiUZClGoZZJ4sqRm\_kBCUs8A\_cSiDjqN4E78RZD3A?e=4cpaNy](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:x:/p/shovan/EU8nHDiUZClGoZZJ4sqRm_kBCUs8A_cSiDjqN4E78RZD3A?e=4cpaNy)
**B: Regression Document:** [RS regression](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:w:/g/personal/sharanya_shetty_ixana_ai/EYI_LkezugpHtJubg6pgI20BFaUPJe1iyMYFnDzra8RmoQ?e=nn5T6L)
**C: GitHub link:** vcs/rs\_testing/verification\_vcs
**How to Run the Testbench** 
1.     1. Change directory to the test environment:  cd /home/SharanyaShetty/digital/qs223/IC-RTL-qs223/vcs/rs\_testing/verification\_vcs 
    2. Compile the RTL testbench using: ./compile.rtl\_sim 
    3. Launch the simulation (with GUI enabled) by running:  ./simv\_rtl -gui & 
**D/E/F/G:** [https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/g/personal/sharanya\_shetty\_ixana\_ai/EcryldIGR\_5BtDixJXNnbKYBQcRpZGe9mGAK6zVE5wMxOA?e=LfZQW6](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/g/personal/sharanya_shetty_ixana_ai/EcryldIGR_5BtDixJXNnbKYBQcRpZGe9mGAK6zVE5wMxOA?e=LfZQW6)

Note: I got KT on July 29 and, in two days before my leave on July 31, completed 90% of test cases with a scalable, code-coverage-ready framework. Blue-highlighted columns in the test plan are covered by regression, execution steps and docs are attached. I’ve shown Souptik how to run the testbench (he’s fixing an err\_gt bug), and I’m reachable by phone for urgent debugging, then will finish the rest when I’m back

comments.text: @Pushpal Das : This task required multiple debug sessions due to design issues, and the server (cluster) where we ran the tests kept failing. As a result, we had to rerun multiple tests repeatedly. There were a total of 154 million tests, which took about one week to complete. During this time, I was working on other tasks while the tests ran in the background. Additionally, I was on leave for 10 days in the middle of this process.
comments.author: Sharanya Shetty

comments.text: Delay Reason: This task was delayed at beginning due to unavailability of @Sharanya Shetty since she was OOO. After she returned, a number of bugs in Testbench and design were encountered. I shared my time in de-bugging Phy at that moment with @Arka Chakraborty, therefore, I couldn't provide my whole time into debugging this task which took more time. After all debugs were done, complete stress testing took more time that expected due to inaccuracy in calculating the time estimate. However, the testing happened in background in the cluster and @Sharanya Shetty was not actively involved in it
comments.author: Souptik Dolui

comments.text: Deadline extended as total run is being done. Cluster and server failure delayed task by 1-2 days as well.
comments.author: Archisman Ghosh

comments.text:
@Archisman Ghosh 
Aug 11–12: Core RS block built and integrated with corner-case checkers.
Aug 13–14: Emulator debug (with Souptik); prepared decks; began fixing the “over-max” (err_gt) issue; planned RS scenario validation. Testbench was ready to test by Aug 14.
Aug 15: Holiday.
Aug 18: Ran post-integration regression; uncovered one RTL issue and one emulator issue; shared in RS Testing & RTL sync; started triage.
Aug 19: Regressions crashed due to Synopsys server overload; filed complaint; consulted Antony and decided to move runs to the cluster. (parallelly working on top level sims)
Aug 20: Set up cluster parallelization; launched overnight test run; gathered waveforms/pointers for follow-up.
Aug 21: Validating results; if green, parallelize full regressions, then proceed to coverage. In parallel, continuing adiabatic verification and AES sims to avoid idle time.

Note: By Aug 14 the testbench was ready to test. During subsequent sweeps we kept uncovering bugs (RTL + emulator). Progress was blocked by server limitations until we migrated to the cluster. Also, Souptik’s bandwidth was limited as he was helping Arka

comments.text: @Pushpal Das : This task required multiple debug sessions due to design issues, and the server (cluster) where we ran the tests kept failing. As a result, we had to rerun multiple tests repeatedly. There were a total of 154 million tests, which took about one week to complete. During this time, I was working on other tasks while the tests ran in the background. Additionally, I was on leave for 10 days in the middle of this process.
comments.author: Sharanya Shetty

comments.text: Delay Reason: This task was delayed at beginning due to unavailability of @Sharanya Shetty since she was OOO. After she returned, a number of bugs in Testbench and design were encountered. I shared my time in de-bugging Phy at that moment with @Arka Chakraborty, therefore, I couldn't provide my whole time into debugging this task which took more time. After all debugs were done, complete stress testing took more time that expected due to inaccuracy in calculating the time estimate. However, the testing happened in background in the cluster and @Sharanya Shetty was not actively involved in it

tasks.name: N3: Effect of AC coupling Zero IF receiver & Acceptable HPF cutoff
tasks.description:
Why : AC coupling systems helps remove effects of DC offsets, for relatively high DR cases the AC coupling doesnt hurt a lot, only if a long strings of 1s or 0s come in a Zero IF system then it starts to degrade performance 

Deliverable : 
1) If AC coupling can work for the current IQ Zero IF arch
2) If yes then what HPF filter cut-off to take (settling time needs to be taken in account & maximum run length to be supported)

Risk
1) Assume some maximum run length (typically 5 or 7), anything higher might need whitening 
2) Settling time for HPF might be modelled inaccurately, affects wake up time & duty cycling power efficiency
Result:
1) Python and mathematical model assuming ED to be simply 'X^2' (X = input across it), leads to AC coupling before ED after mixer to be infeasible.
2) Discussions with Aniruddh C and Sumon : ED model maybe inaccurate, need to update it and check in Synopsys sim
tasks.blockerReason:
Delay Reason @pm:
1. Summary: AC coupling system is not feasible for the current IQ Zero IF architecture due to inaccurate ED model.
2. Technical: Inaccurate ED model leads to infeasibility of AC coupling before ED after mixer, requiring update and verification in Synopsys sim.

tasks.name: N3: Ramp up - Compare homodyne & hetrodyne Rx arch performance
tasks.description:
Why : Heterodyne arch
          1) uses single 50% duty cycled mixer (easier to design clocking, less power)
          2) can have AC coupled system (upto ED), easier offset correction 
          3) I-Q matching not required, easier layout & matching considerations 
          4) Uses >2x Data B/W for TIA, LPF & ED, but uses only single path 
          5) Easier to match impedance from antenna 
          6) Initial theory predicts 3db poorer sensitivity, Subodh's results would prove/disprove the theory 
          7) Requires Tx & Rx DCO to have well defined Freq offset (need finer freq steps than Fd)  
8) Requires image reject filters (high side or low side depending on sign of freq offset used), Might be prohibitively expensive in a FHSS system, but we might be able to get away without it (Needs discussion)

Limitations: Image rejection problem for heterodyne arch needs deeper discussion 

 Homodyne arch
          1) uses two 25% duty cycled mixer (difficult to design clocking, more power)
          2) DC coupled system, offset correction needs to be checked
          3) I-Q matching required, layout & matching considerations 
          4) Uses ~1x Data B/W for TIA, LPF & ED, but uses two paths 
          5) Needs complex impedance matching, harder than matching real impedance (using single mixer) 
          6) Requires Tx & Rx DCO to have low Freq offset (Step size = Fd) 

Deliverable 
1) BER vs Attenuation plots for 2 arch at 0 offset (best case)
2) BER vs Attenuation plots 3 arch across phase & freq offset errors 

Limitation 
Modelling error might be present, might need a review 

Risk
Slight delay can be expect as Subodh is still new

tasks.name: N3: Selection of Initial architecture
tasks.description:
**Why:** Ixana plans to integrate on chip core. Potential candidates are ARM/RISC V/ or Synopsys?
**Deliverable:** a) Pros and con discussion with Shreyas and Shovan. b) Discussion what other MCU/Bluetooth and UWB SoC do c) Analysis of open source RISC V processors d) Final architecture mention
**Risks (T,E):**
(T) NA
(E)
**Limitations** (Assumptions (A), Constraints (C), Dependency (D))
(A) NA
(C) NA
(D) Might spend some time to check document or unblock Aneesh on FPGA setup
**Results** (to be updated after task is complete):
a. PPT/doc link:   [https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/r/personal/archisman\_ghosh\_ixana\_ai/\_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B32817581-3772-4886-B0B1-15BA6474323B%7D&file=CPU%20exploration%20RnD%20for%20QS222SOC.pptx&action=edit&mobileredirect=true](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/r/personal/archisman_ghosh_ixana_ai/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B32817581-3772-4886-B0B1-15BA6474323B%7D&file=CPU%20exploration%20RnD%20for%20QS222SOC.pptx&action=edit&mobileredirect=true)
b. As per discussion with Shreyas/Shovan we will proceed with ARM core.

| bluetooth/UWB SoC | Core | Freq | SPI | QSPI/HSSPI | I2C | I2S | UART | Security | RAM | ROM | NVM | DMA | JTAG | SWD |
| ---| ---| ---| ---| ---| ---| ---| ---| ---| ---| ---| ---| ---| ---| --- |
| Atmosic | ARM Cortex33F w DSP | 64MHz | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y | Y | 128KB | 64KB | 512KB | Y | N | N |
| Decawave DW1000 | N | N | Y | N | N | N | N | N | N | N | N | N | N | N |
| Giant Microsemi Gt1500 UWB | N | N | Y | N | Y | N | Y | N | N | N | N | N | N | N |
| QORVO 35825 QM SoC | ARM M33 MCU | Not mentioned | Y | Y | N | N | Y | Y | NM | NM | NM | N | Y | Y |
| Silicon Lab SoC Class | ARM core | Not mentioned | Y | N | Y | N | N | Y | 512KB | N | 0/2/4MB | Y | Y | Y |

(T)iger is a risk that everybody understands
(E)lephant is a risk that I know but others don't think so, yet
**AG comments:**
Update 19June:
Here is an update about different IPs: 
1. **Core**: ARM documents are a bit difficult to follow. In general, there will be a way to interface with  RAM through AHB bus. They don't have much options to tightly-coupled memory.  
2. **Flash memory**: ARM provides flash controller IP. SO, it could be easier to interface with external or internal flash. But at this point, I am trying to gather more info. I don't have full clarity. Shovan Maity I need help from you may be. Synopsys does not have reasonable flash IP either.
3. **SiFIve**: Followed up with them about the NDA to know if they have something more to offer. Initial discussion was not impressive to me. 
**Update 25June**
1. **Core**: They did not respond anything yet. I have followed up with them again today. 
2. **Flash memory**: Shovan/me discussed. For 65nm, not much option of off chip memory. We have decided on off chip route. Shared document to Shovan about potential options. Interface has to be SPI. 
3. **MIPI**: Mixel seems a good option but they will not be able to help for October TO. Arasan replied. Scheduled a meeting next week. 
4. **SiFIve**: NDA signed. 
5. **AES Interface**: Archisman to work on final interface and discuss with Sovan. requesting to check the final architecture in the previously shared slides. Shreyas Sen: we are waiting on key sharing and use case. Need this info to finalize. 
**Update 15July**
1. **Core**: ARM has provided the IP. However, they are supposed to walk us through the different options. We are able to start this.
2. **Flash memory**: Shovan/me discussed. For 65nm, not much option of off chip memory. We have decided on off chip route. Shared document to Shovan about potential options. Interface has to be SPI. 
3. **MIPI**: Arasan quote suggests 0.8M. Discussion ongoing. We have a soft decision towards internal IP development. Angik is still talking.
4. **SiFIve**: NDA signed. They don't provide peripherals. PPA number is worse, so we will not be pursuing.
[@Shovan Maity](#user_mention#75401028) will this task continue till we found final architecture code? If yes, we can keep it open but needs to adjust the timeline.

AI Archisman: If they support I2C now?
Intention to have audio demo....
SWD .. update this task..
**1st August;**
Final PPT : [https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/r/personal/archisman\_ghosh\_ixana\_ai/\_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B32817581-3772-4886-B0B1-15BA6474323B%7D&file=CPU%20exploration%20RnD%20for%20QS222SOC.pptx&action=edit&mobileredirect=true](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/r/personal/archisman_ghosh_ixana_ai/_layouts/15/Doc.aspx?sourcedoc=%7B32817581-3772-4886-B0B1-15BA6474323B%7D&file=CPU%20exploration%20RnD%20for%20QS222SOC.pptx&action=edit&mobileredirect=true)
Handdrawn architecture updated.

tasks.name: RS existing algorithm Python Model
tasks.description:
Why: No existing Python model is available till now. It is needed to decode the RS encoded signal, and the internal signal of different RTL modules can be compared with a Python simulation.
deliverable: a) exactly model the present RS algorithm in Python. b) create a task in reviewers name with same description for review. c) Slide deck with point 1-8.
Risk(T,E):
E: NA
T: If BM-KES module is not modeled exactly, it will be difficult to pinpoint the issue of present RS algorithm
Limitation: (A,C,D)
assumption: The inversion-less BM-KES Python version is available on the internet.

1. Look through theory papers and existing python scripts of RS
2\. Implement RS Encoder
3\. Test RS Encoder and compare with RTL
4\. Implement RS Decoder and modularise like RTL
5\. Plot internal signal of KES
6\. Compare with RTL with same error magnitude and positions
7\. Make relevant changes in RTL

raw.name: RS existing algorithm Python Model
raw.description:
Why: No existing Python model is available till now. It is needed to decode the RS encoded signal, and the internal signal of different RTL modules can be compared with a Python simulation.
deliverable: a) exactly model the present RS algorithm in Python. b) create a task in reviewers name with same description for review. c) Slide deck with point 1-8.
Risk(T,E):
E: NA
T: If BM-KES module is not modeled exactly, it will be difficult to pinpoint the issue of present RS algorithm
Limitation: (A,C,D)
assumption: The inversion-less BM-KES Python version is available on the internet.

1. Look through theory papers and existing python scripts of RS
2\. Implement RS Encoder
3\. Test RS Encoder and compare with RTL
4\. Implement RS Decoder and modularise like RTL
5\. Plot internal signal of KES
6\. Compare with RTL with same error magnitude and positions
7\. Make relevant changes in RTL
8\. Test python and RTL and compare result

tasks.description:

tasks.name: N3: ED bandwidth (settling time)
tasks.description:
Why: Need spec for EDs analog implementation.
Deliverables: Bandwidth and selling time required for ED
Risk: ED arch may have to change
Limitation: Improper ED modeling can lead to improper spec and other non-linearity may affect overall performance
Result: ED bandwidth to be 100MHz using heterodyne architecture. FD=40M, IF=40M , so min ED BW=80MHz. So 100MHz with margin.

comments.text: We were desiding on Homodyne and Heterodyne architecture and ED BW depends on that.
comments.author: KiritkumarP

**Lint:**

2. put the command: vc\_static\_shell and start\_gui

**CDC:**
1. go to: /home/SharanyaShetty/digital/qs223/IC-RTL-qs223/cdc/apbi2c
2. put the command: vc\_static\_shell and start\_gui

comments.text: This task has been completed on 29th September when I explained @Sharanya Shetty how to build the emulator of PS based on output signals of PS. Since then@Sharanya Shetty has completed building the emulator of PS for FHSS with the help of Sayantan and the verification of FHSS has been completed.

comments.text: Initial meeting with @Sharanya Shetty completed today. I have showed her one of the PS tests she can use as reference. For now, the following signals: sync_done and slot_done are not present in PS Top level module. 2 other signals can be used as reference for the same. I have showed that to @Sharanya Shetty as well

tasks.name: N3: M0 subsystem failed tests debug
tasks.description:
Few tests are shown : Running M0 subsystem and sharing to FW team ([https://app.clickup.com/t/86dxw7nee](https://app.clickup.com/t/86dxw7nee))
Failed test to be debugged along with new ones.
## Lists of Non-passing Tests
1. [APB Mux Tests](https://app.clickup.com/t/86dy6vrwd?block=block-56063756-af74-46a1-a797-c6a6947f7a25) (PASSED)
2. Debug Tests (PASSED)
3. Dhry (PASSED)
4. DMA Tests (PASSED)
5. GPIO Tests (PASSED)
6. Interrupt Demo (PASSED)
7. Romtable Tests (PASSED)
8. SSP Tests (PASSED)

**Source code and execuables:** [https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-FPGA-qs223\_m0/tree/main/Behavioural\_tests](https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-FPGA-qs223_m0/tree/main/Behavioural_tests)
# Debugging
## APB Mux Tests (PASSED)
*   Since up to 12 ports (0:11) work on the mux with the port to enable SPI (SSp) blocking the rest of the tests, I disabled this in the cmsdk\_apb\_subsytem initialization in cmsdk\_mcu\_system.v
    *   This are the resulting paramter settings
    ```scss
      // APB subsystem for timers, UARTs, SPI
      cmsdk_apb_subsystem #(
        .APB_EXT_PORT12_ENABLE   (0),  // Include for SPI interface
        .APB_EXT_PORT13_ENABLE   (1),
        .APB_EXT_PORT14_ENABLE   (1),
        .APB_EXT_PORT15_ENABLE   (INCLUDE_DMA),
        .INCLUDE_IRQ_SYNCHRONIZER(0),
        .INCLUDE_APB_TEST_SLAVE  (1),
        .INCLUDE_APB_TIMER0      (1),  // Include simple timer #0
        .INCLUDE_APB_TIMER1      (1),  // Include simple timer #1
        .INCLUDE_APB_DUALTIMER0  (1),  // Include dual timer module
        .INCLUDE_APB_UART0       (1),  // Include simple UART #0
        .INCLUDE_APB_UART1       (1),  // Include simple UART #1
        .INCLUDE_APB_UART2       (1),  // Include simple UART #2.
        .INCLUDE_APB_WATCHDOG    (1),  // Include APB watchdog module
        .BE                      (BE)
         )
    ```
*   Rerunning the test in simulation, I see this

```yaml
1419191 ns UART: 12: APB expansion port 12 
1468271 ns UART:   All ID values are 0   : device not present 
1476231 ns UART:  
1510471 ns UART: 13: APB expansion port 13 
1620731 ns UART: Difference found: 4000dfd0, expected 0, actual 4 
1732311 ns UART: Difference found: 4000dfe0, expected 0, actual 22 
1842931 ns UART: Difference found: 4000dfe4, expected 0, actual b8

3401871 ns UART:   ID value mismatch(es) : device unknown

3462671 ns UART: 15: APB expansion port 15 (optional DMA controller) 
3576851 ns UART: Difference found: 4000ffe0, expected 30, actual 22 
3688791 ns UART: Difference found: 4000ffe4, expected b2, actual b8 
3732211 ns UART:   ID value mismatch(es) : device unknown 
3740171 ns UART:  
3768511 ns UART:  
3820011 ns UART: ** TEST FAILED **, Error code = (0xe000) 
3823531 ns UART: Test Ended
```

*   In inspecting the source code it seems there are 12 expected IDs in particular locations offset from the peripheral base addresses.
    *   For example, observering the last PORT 15 connect to the DMA, 10/12 IDs match the expected with differences found at `0x4000ffe0, expected 0x30, actual 0x22` and `0x4000ffe4, expected 0xb2, actual 0xb8` . In the test source code, I can change the expected ID array to match what is currently in those address locations for each peripheral, but I don't know the significance of those IDs.
        *   This is the ID array for PORT15 for the DMA (using last 12 words as IDs):
        *   {0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, **0x04, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x30, 0xB2, 0x0B, 0x00, 0x0D, 0xF0, 0x05, 0xB1**};
    *   I came back to this test after succesfully integrating the dma device with the **_cmsdk\_apb\_subsytem.v_** file by matching the expected IDs in simulation
    *   Since timer 2 and timer 3 are being used in the external apb ports 13 and 14 on the slave mux, I changed the expected id in the test source code (which was set to blank\_id to use the apb\_timer\_id instead):
    ```cpp
    const unsigned char apb_timer_id[16]    = {0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x04, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00,
                                               0x22, 0xB8, 0x0B, 0x00, 0x0D, 0xF0, 0x05, 0xB1};
    ...
    const unsigned char blank_id[16]        = {0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00,
                                               0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00};
    ```

```cpp
  puts ("13: timer 2");
    if ( ID_Check(&apb_timer_id[0],         0x4000D000          ) == 1 ) err_code |= 1<<13;
  puts ("14: timer 3");
    if ( ID_Check(&apb_timer_id[0],         0x4000E000          ) == 1 ) err_code |= 1<<14;
```

*       *   Omiting the check for the Ssp module the test passes, need to verify Ssp integration

```yaml
107279 ns UART: CMSDK Boot Loader 
111779 ns UART: - load flash 
261859 ns UART:  
306619 ns UART: Cortex Microcontroller System Design Kit 
355639 ns UART:  - APB slave mux test - revision $Revision: 371321 $ 
363599 ns UART:  
412019 ns UART:  - Detecting if default peripherals are present ...

1051999 ns UART:

1124119 ns UART:   All ID values matched : device present 
1132079 ns UART:

1203659 ns UART:   All ID values are 0   : device not present 
1211619 ns UART:  
1235359 ns UART: 10: blank 
1284439 ns UART:   All ID values are 0   : device not present 
1292399 ns UART:  
1321979 ns UART: 11: APB test slave 
1377059 ns UART:   APB test slave test passed 
1385019 ns UART:  
1419199 ns UART: 12: APB expansion port 12 
1468279 ns UART:   All ID values are 0   : device not present 
1476239 ns UART:  
1502099 ns UART: 13: timer 2 
1548339 ns UART:   All ID values matched : device present 
1556299 ns UART:  
1582139 ns UART: 14: timer 3 
1628379 ns UART:   All ID values matched : device present 
1636339 ns UART:  
1689119 ns UART: 15: APB expansion port 15 (optional DMA controller) 
1735379 ns UART:   All ID values matched : device present 
1743339 ns UART:  
1757019 ns UART:  
1783139 ns UART: ** TEST PASSED ** 
1785879 ns UART: Test Ended
```

*   In debugging the Ssp integration, there is a mismatch in the expected size of `PRDATA` (i.e. 16-bits \[15:0\])on the Ssp side as well as on the apb\_slave\_mux (i.e. 32-bits \[31:0\])
*   Because of this mismatch, the upper 16 bits were left unassigned, and having uninitialized signals into the mux blocked reading from the address locations sent by the apb\_mux\_tests
    *   The program would suspend during the ID\_Check call
    *   In the _cmsdk\_apb\_slave\_mux_ instantiation in **_cmsdk\_apb\_subsystem.v_**, I concatenated 0s to the 16-bit ssp\_prdata from the Ssp module
    ```scss
        .PSEL12            (ssp_psel),
        .PREADY12          (ssp_pready),
        .PRDATA12          ({16'h0000, ssp_prdata}),
        .PSLVERR12         (ssp_pslverr),
    ```
*   This then unblocked the test and the ID checks ran to completion
*   Since this test compares a predefined ID vector for each peripheral to verify the presence of the devices, additional peripherals being added are external to the test. An ID vector for the additional device must be added to the software test.
    *   I was able to obtain the IDs for the Ssp device by directly reading these values based on the address locations of the Ssp's data, these also matched the Peripheral Identification Register Value constants in **_SspDefs.v_** at the expected address locations (offset from 0x4000C000 which is the 4kB baseaddress allocated for APB expansion port 12)
    *   I added this vector as part of the test apb\_mux\_tests.c:
    ```cpp
    const unsigned char ssp_id[16]          = {0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00,
                                               0x22, 0x10, 0x34, 0x00, 0x0D, 0xF0, 0x05, 0xB1};
    ...
    ```

*   Though the DEBUGTESTER macro should be able to read a valid address location at 0x0 from observing the debug\_tests.hex, it seems the program still stalls.

```cpp
// CM0 MCU's view of the memory shared with the debugtester
// (4 words above stack top)
// This macro uses the SP value from the vector table as stacktop
// The stacktop can not be set to the top of the memory.
#define DEBUGTESTERDATA ((volatile uint32_t *) *((uint32_t *) 0x0))
```

*   Instead of reading the MSP value at 0x0, this was replaced with the actual value of the MSP from the debug\_tests.hex program, which was 0x2000FC00 in `debug_test.h`

```cpp
// CM0 MCU's view of the memory shared with the debugtester
// (4 words above stack top)
// This macro uses the SP value from the vector table as stacktop
// The stacktop can not be set to the top of the memory.
#define DEBUGTESTERDATA ((volatile uint32_t *) 0x2000FC00)
```

*   Some updates concerning the **uDMA simple test:**
    *   Looking at the timing diagrams, the issue does not begin at the dma interfacing, but early on in the process of copying the contents of some source array into a destination array.
    *   In the test source code, a `source_data_array` and `dest_data_array` are initialized from 0 to 3 and 0 respectively.
    *   I've been able to trace this step in simulation by monitoring the writes to the SRAM at the address locations of the arrays.
    *   Below is a summary of the array locations:
    ```plain
    // Source Array
    [0] 200006ec
    [1] 200006f0
    [2] 200006f4
    [3] 200006f8
    // Destination Array
    [0] 200006fc
    [1] 20000700
    [2] 20000704
    [3] 20000708
    ```
*   Below is an image of the first stage of the dma transaction (blue arrows for dest\_array init and red for source array init)

![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/b60914e1-9583-48a4-87fe-4c1cc8c114b5/image.png)

*   There is a one cycle latency between writes to the SRAM and reads, so in this test the arrays are initially populated and written to the SRAM location as shown in the diagram above.
*   Then this data is sent to the ahb bus via the `ahb_slave_mux`.
    *   sram\_hrdata and sram\_hsel are inputs to this module, I'm expecting the same data to be available on the output of the mux before entering the ahb\_master\_mux and eventually dma
    *   For some reason, there is an issue with moving the data through the `ahb_slave_mux`
*   At first I thought the issue might be the additional pipelining stage in the synchronous SRAM module between read and writes, but I've tried removing/adding various pipelining stages, from the generation of sram\_hsel to the processing of sram\_hsel in the slave mux, and also modifying the behavioural SRAM model by removing the pipelining of reads, but this would break things
*   Since other transactions with the SRAM work as expected, I think the synchronous transaction between the SRAM and AHB bus is necessary.
*   I tried comparing the timing of one of these transactions between loading the SRAM and reading these values at the output of the `ahb_slave_mux`, and the timing behaviour does match what I see for the case of the sram transaction in the dma test, but the cycle offset where `sram_hsel` asserts and when the value of sram\_hrdata is available on the input of the mux differ by 1 cycle.
*   So then the wrong data is written to the `dmac_hrdata`, I'm not sure how to resolve this
*   According to the documentation, the transactions between the SRAM and AHB bus is behaving as expected, what I observe is similar to what the following timing diagram indicates:

![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/0fa5024a-6213-458e-8457-c905019838fe/image.png)

```sql
    result += gpio_register_rw_test(CMSDK_GPIO0);
    result += gpio_interrupt_test(CMSDK_GPIO0);
```

*   At the end of the gpio\_register\_rw\_test(CMSDK\_GPIO0), err\_code = 0x1F (hexadecimal), that is binary 0000000000011111.
    *   This means bits 0-4 are set, which corresponds to individual IRQs 16 through 20 not being pending when expected after the test stimulus.
    *   IRQs 21 to 31 (bits 5-15) were pending as expected (their bits are not set in err\_code).
    *   Modified the test to check the individual interrupts on GPIO0 for IRQs set at \[21:31\] and the test passed
        *   So there is an issue with the GPIO0 interrupts for IRQ \[16:20\], i.e., PORT0\_1 to PORT0\_4
*   Went back and checked the interrupt assignments in the verilog, looks like IRQ \[16:20\] has been borrowed to assign interrupts for the newly integrated Ssp (SPI) interface in **_cmsdk\_apb\_subsystem.v_**

```verilog
assign apbsubsys_interrupt[31:0] = {
                   {11{1'b0}},                       // 21-31 (AHB GPIO #0 individual interrupt)
                   i_ssp_txintr,                     // 20 // SSP interrupt added
                   i_ssp_rxintr,                     // 19 // SSP interrupt added
                   i_ssp_rorintr,                    // 18 // SSP interrupt added
                   i_ssp_rtintr,                     // 17 // SSP interrupt added
                   i_ssp_intr,                       // 16 //SSP interrupt added
                   1'b0,                             // 15 (DMA interrupt)
                   i_uart2_overflow_int,             // 14
                   i_uart1_overflow_int,             // 13
                   i_uart0_overflow_int,             // 12
                   1'b0,                             // 11
                   i_dualtimer2_int,                 // 10
                   i_timer1_int,                     // 9
                   i_timer0_int,                     // 8
                   1'b0,                             // 7 (GPIO #1 combined interrupt)
                   1'b0,                             // 6 (GPIO #0 combined interrupt)
                   i_uart2_txint | i_timer4_int,                    // 5 -- timer interrupts, might need to change it
                   i_uart2_rxint | i_timer3_int,                    // 4 -- timer interrupts, might need to change it
                   i_uart1_txint | i_timer2_int,                    // 3 -- timer interrupts, might need to change it
                   i_uart1_rxint,                    // 2
                   i_uart0_txint,                    // 1
                   i_uart0_rxint};                   // 0
```

*   Then in the next level of hierachhy (**_cmsdk\_mcu\_system.v_**), the assignments to the ISR did not match the assignments that this test expects

```verilog
// -------------------------------
  // Interrupt assignment
  // -------------------------------

  assign intnmi_cm0        = watchdog_interrupt;
  assign intisr_cm0[ 5: 0] = apbsubsys_interrupt[ 5: 0];
  assign intisr_cm0[ 6]    = apbsubsys_interrupt[ 6]   | gpio0_combintr;
  assign intisr_cm0[ 7]    = apbsubsys_interrupt[ 7]   | gpio1_combintr;
  assign intisr_cm0[14: 8] = apbsubsys_interrupt[14: 8];
  assign intisr_cm0[15]    = apbsubsys_interrupt[15]   | dma_done | dma_err;
  assign intisr_cm0[20:16] = apbsubsys_interrupt[20:16];
  assign intisr_cm0[31:21] = apbsubsys_interrupt[31:21]| gpio0_intr;
```

*   This is the new Interrupt assignment section in **_cmsdk\_mcu\_system.v_**

```verilog
// -------------------------------
  // Interrupt assignment
  // -------------------------------

  assign intnmi_cm0        = watchdog_interrupt;
  assign intisr_cm0[ 5: 0] = apbsubsys_interrupt[ 5: 0];
  assign intisr_cm0[ 6]    = apbsubsys_interrupt[ 6]   | gpio0_combintr;
  assign intisr_cm0[ 7]    = apbsubsys_interrupt[ 7]   | gpio1_combintr;
  assign intisr_cm0[14: 8] = apbsubsys_interrupt[14: 8];
  assign intisr_cm0[15]    = apbsubsys_interrupt[15]   | dma_done | dma_err;
  assign intisr_cm0[20:16] = apbsubsys_interrupt[20:16] | gpio0_intr[4:0];
  assign intisr_cm0[31:21] = apbsubsys_interrupt[31:21]| gpio0_intr[15:5];
```

## Romtable Tests (PASSED)
*   Program stalls at the call to EnableDebugTester()
    *   In particular, at the end of the function, the values at the top of the stack memory are set to 0. There is an important constrain from the documentation that specifies that the top of stack memory must be at least 4 four 32-bit words (i.e., 16 bytes) lower than the top of SRAM.
    *   The debug tester locates the Main Stack Pointer at address 0x0 in the ROM
    *   Though the DEBUGTESTER macro should be able to read a valid address location at 0x0 from observing the romtable.hex, it seems the program still stalls.
    ```cpp
    // CMSDK example MCU's view of the memory shared with the debugtester
    // (4 words above stack top)
    // This macro uses the SP value from the vector table as stacktop
    // The stacktop cannot be set to the top of the memory.
    #define DEBUGTESTERDATA ((volatile uint32_t *) *((uint32_t *) 0x0))
    ```
    *   Instead of reading the MSP value at 0x0, this was replaced with the actual value of the MSP from the romtable.hex program, which was 0x2000FC00 in `mcu_debugtester_interface.h`
    ```cpp
    // CMSDK example MCU's view of the memory shared with the debugtester
    // (4 words above stack top)
    // This macro uses the SP value from the vector table as stacktop
    // The stacktop cannot be set to the top of the memory.
    #define DEBUGTESTERDATA ((volatile uint32_t *) 0x2000FC00)
    ```
    *   Doing this the application successfully enabled the debug tester
    *   Testbench perspective: StartDebugTester()
        *   First, pins\[13:8\] of CMSDK\_GPIO0 is set to the output direction
        *   `0010 0001 1111 1111 or 0x21ff` is writtent to P0
        *   Pins \[13:8\] controls the DBG\_COMMAND
        *   DBG\_COMMAND is used to select the function type from the enum function table (currently reading 1 i.e., FnSetInterfaceSW)
        ```cpp
        enum Function { FnSetInterfaceJTAG,
        		FnSetInterfaceSW,
        		FnDAPPowerUp,
        		FnDAPPowerDown,
        		FnGetTAPID,
        		FnGetDPReg,
        		FnGetAPReg,
        		FnGetAPMem,
        		FnSetAPMem,
        		FnGetAPMemCSIDs,
        		FnConnectWakeUnhalt,
        		FnConnectCheckUnlockup,
        		FnEnableHaltingDebug,
        		FnDAPAccess,
        		FnConfigTrace,
        		FnCheckTrace
        };
        ```
        *   Right now, the issue is with outputs from the gpio\_0\_out signal and gpio\_0\_en are not being driven
            *   I guess **debugtester\_le.hex** program is supposed to control this
        *   Hence, DBGRUNNING is never asserted along with other pins connected to this internal port
        *   Issue seems to be connecting the testbench and the debug tester module (cmsdk\_debug\_tester.v)
            *   debugtester\_le.hex program sets the debug interface, which is needed in the function call StartDebugTester(), otherwise the program suspends in this infinite while loop
            ```cpp
              // Wait to see Running asserted
              while((CMSDK_GPIO0->DATA & DEBUG_RUNNING) == 0); // right now, DEBUG_RUNNING is not set because enables on tristate are not set
            ```
        *   I'm going to move on to debugging other tests as it seems this test does not block or causes dependencies on the functions of the core
        *   Came back to this issue and found the bug:
            *   In cmsdk\_debug\_tester.v, there are similar modules instantiated as is the case for cmsdk\_mcu
            ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/58a9be0a-06aa-4d6f-aaff-a675a380c5da/image.png)
            *   Since the **debugtester\_le.hex** is used in concert with the romtable test, I thought to look into the rom module to ensure the program is loaded properly
            *   What I found in simulation was the control signals used to interface the ROM module was in an undefined state
            *   I looked more closely at the source code for cmsdk\_debug\_tester.v and found that **Cortex-M Integration** wrapper which instatiates the CM0 core itself as well as other wrappers for debug was not instantiated
            *   I added `` `include "cmsdk_mcu_defs.v" `` to add the **Cortex-M Integration** as shown in the diagram above and the remainder of the romtable test passsed.

## SSP Tests (PASSED)
*   Tried to compile the tests from the SSP package, including some API source files from ARM but was not successful
*   Decided to write some baremetal tests directly, the list of current tests includes
    *   A peripheral ID test (`pl022_ssp_detect(baseaddr)`): This test looks at the peripheral and component ID registers, ARM has hardcoded particular bytes to identify the SSP device, being able to read the values at these register locations and comparing them with the expected values from the manufacturer will validate that the device has been correctly integrated into the apb peripheral.
    *   Read/Write test (`ssp_register_rw_test(baseaddr)`): This test writes to control register 0 and reads from the same address as control register 0. The test passes if the value written is the value read back.
    *   Functional loopback test (`ssp_functional_loopback_test(baseaddr)`): This test configures the SSP interface in Motorolla SPI format and writes a 16-bit value to the 16-bit data register (SSPDR), in Loop back mode (enabled in the SSPCR1) the output of the transmit serial shifter is connected to the input of the receive serial shifter internally. So once the Tx fifo is empty the test value is written, and similarly when the Rx fifo is not empty, the value in the receive FIFO is read back.
    *   Extended loopback test (`ssp_extended_loopback_buffer_test(baseaddr)`): This test is similar to the functional loopback test, except multiple reads and writes are performed based on the size of the Tx and Rx buffers.
    *   Interrupt tests (`ssp_interrupt_tests(baseaddr)`): This test looks to trigger the four ssp interrupts:
        *   SSPRXINTR: triggered when there are four or more valid entries in the receive FIFO (connected to IRQ 19)
        *   SSPTXINTR: triggered when there are four or fewer valid entries in the transmit FIFO (connected to IRQ 20)
        *   SSPRORINTR: triggered when the receive FIFO is already full and an additional data frame is received causing an additional data fram is received causing an overrun of the FIFO. Data is over-written in the receive shift register, but not at the FIFO (connected to IRQ 18)
        *   SSPRTINTR: triggered when the receive FIFO is not empty and the SSP has been idle for a fixed 32-bit period. Cleared when new data is received on SSPRXD or if the receive FIFO becomes empty by subsequent reads (connected to IRQ 17)
        *   SSPINTR: a combined interrupt, i.e., an OR function of the four individual interrupts above (connected to IRQ 16)
        *   which have also been connected to IRQ

```yaml
3436379 ns UART: Wrote 0x51 to Tx buffer, waiting for Rx... 
3510399 ns UART: Wrote 0x52 to Tx buffer, waiting for Rx...

6350339 ns UART:  
6365799 ns UART:  
6421259 ns UART: ** TEST PASSED **: SSP is addressable and functional. 
6423999 ns UART: Test Ended
```

**NOTE:** To pass the \[6\] RX Timeout Interrupt Test, clock gating must be disabled (i.e. ``//`define ARM_CMSDK_INCLUDE_CLKGATE`` ) in the `cmsdk_mcu_defs.v` file. During periods of inactivity where the Ssp is disabled, the Ssp clock is also disabled with clock gating to conserve power. This means the value of the receive fifo is empty and interferes with the timeout interrupt assertion/desassertion conditions.

raw.name: N3: M0 subsystem failed tests debug
raw.description:
Few tests are shown : Running M0 subsystem and sharing to FW team ([https://app.clickup.com/t/86dxw7nee](https://app.clickup.com/t/86dxw7nee))
Failed test to be debugged along with new ones.
## Lists of Non-passing Tests
1. [APB Mux Tests](https://app.clickup.com/t/86dy6vrwd?block=block-56063756-af74-46a1-a797-c6a6947f7a25) (PASSED)
2. Debug Tests (PASSED)
3. Dhry (PASSED)
4. DMA Tests (PASSED)
5. GPIO Tests (PASSED)
6. Interrupt Demo (PASSED)
7. Romtable Tests (PASSED)
8. SSP Tests (PASSED)

**Source code and execuables:** [https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-FPGA-qs223\_m0/tree/main/Behavioural\_tests](https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-FPGA-qs223_m0/tree/main/Behavioural_tests)
# Debugging
## APB Mux Tests (PASSED)
*   Since up to 12 ports (0:11) work on the mux with the port to enable SPI (SSp) blocking the rest of the tests, I disabled this in the cmsdk\_apb\_subsytem initialization in cmsdk\_mcu\_system.v
    *   This are the resulting paramter settings
    ```scss
      // APB subsystem for timers, UARTs, SPI
      cmsdk_apb_subsystem #(
        .APB_EXT_PORT12_ENABLE   (0),  // Include for SPI interface
        .APB_EXT_PORT13_ENABLE   (1),
        .APB_EXT_PORT14_ENABLE   (1),
        .APB_EXT_PORT15_ENABLE   (INCLUDE_DMA),
        .INCLUDE_IRQ_SYNCHRONIZER(0),
        .INCLUDE_APB_TEST_SLAVE  (1),
        .INCLUDE_APB_TIMER0      (1),  // Include simple timer #0
        .INCLUDE_APB_TIMER1      (1),  // Include simple timer #1
        .INCLUDE_APB_DUALTIMER0  (1),  // Include dual timer module
        .INCLUDE_APB_UART0       (1),  // Include simple UART #0
        .INCLUDE_APB_UART1       (1),  // Include simple UART #1
        .INCLUDE_APB_UART2       (1),  // Include simple UART #2.
        .INCLUDE_APB_WATCHDOG    (1),  // Include APB watchdog module
        .BE                      (BE)
         )
    ```
*   Rerunning the test in simulation, I see this

```yaml
1419191 ns UART: 12: APB expansion port 12 
1468271 ns UART:   All ID values are 0   : device not present 
1476231 ns UART:  
1510471 ns UART: 13: APB expansion port 13 
1620731 ns UART: Difference found: 4000dfd0, expected 0, actual 4 
1732311 ns UART: Difference found: 4000dfe0, expected 0, actual 22 
1842931 ns UART: Difference found: 4000dfe4, expected 0, actual b8 
1953671 ns UART: Difference found: 4000dfe8, expected 0, actual 1b 
2063151 ns UART: Difference found: 4000dff0, expected 0, actual d 
2173751 ns UART: Difference found: 4000dff4, expected 0, actual f0 
2283031 ns UART: Difference found: 4000dff8, expected 0, actual 5 
2393611 ns UART: Difference found: 4000dffc, expected 0, actual b1 
2435091 ns UART:   ID value mismatch(es) : device unknown 
2443051 ns UART:  
2477251 ns UART: 14: APB expansion port 14 
2587511 ns UART: Difference found: 4000efd0, expected 0, actual 4 
2699091 ns UART: Difference found: 4000efe0, expected 0, actual 22 
2809711 ns UART: Difference found: 4000efe4, expected 0, actual b8 
2920451 ns UART: Difference found: 4000efe8, expected 0, actual 1b

3140531 ns UART: Difference found: 4000eff4, expected 0, actual f0

3401871 ns UART:   ID value mismatch(es) : device unknown 
3409831 ns UART:  
3462671 ns UART: 15: APB expansion port 15 (optional DMA controller) 
3576851 ns UART: Difference found: 4000ffe0, expected 30, actual 22 
3688791 ns UART: Difference found: 4000ffe4, expected b2, actual b8 
3732211 ns UART:   ID value mismatch(es) : device unknown 
3740171 ns UART:  
3768511 ns UART:  
3820011 ns UART: ** TEST FAILED **, Error code = (0xe000) 
3823531 ns UART: Test Ended
```

*   In inspecting the source code it seems there are 12 expected IDs in particular locations offset from the peripheral base addresses.
    *   For example, observering the last PORT 15 connect to the DMA, 10/12 IDs match the expected with differences found at `0x4000ffe0, expected 0x30, actual 0x22` and `0x4000ffe4, expected 0xb2, actual 0xb8` . In the test source code, I can change the expected ID array to match what is currently in those address locations for each peripheral, but I don't know the significance of those IDs.
        *   This is the ID array for PORT15 for the DMA (using last 12 words as IDs):
        *   {0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, **0x04, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x30, 0xB2, 0x0B, 0x00, 0x0D, 0xF0, 0x05, 0xB1**};
    *   I came back to this test after succesfully integrating the dma device with the **_cmsdk\_apb\_subsytem.v_** file by matching the expected IDs in simulation
    *   Since timer 2 and timer 3 are being used in the external apb ports 13 and 14 on the slave mux, I changed the expected id in the test source code (which was set to blank\_id to use the apb\_timer\_id instead):
    ```cpp
    const unsigned char apb_timer_id[16]    = {0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x04, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00,
                                               0x22, 0xB8, 0x0B, 0x00, 0x0D, 0xF0, 0x05, 0xB1};
    ...
    const unsigned char blank_id[16]        = {0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00,
                                               0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00};
    ```

```cpp
  puts ("13: timer 2");
    if ( ID_Check(&apb_timer_id[0],         0x4000D000          ) == 1 ) err_code |= 1<<13;
  puts ("14: timer 3");
    if ( ID_Check(&apb_timer_id[0],         0x4000E000          ) == 1 ) err_code |= 1<<14;
```

*       *   Omiting the check for the Ssp module the test passes, need to verify Ssp integration


*   In debugging the Ssp integration, there is a mismatch in the expected size of `PRDATA` (i.e. 16-bits \[15:0\])on the Ssp side as well as on the apb\_slave\_mux (i.e. 32-bits \[31:0\])
*   Because of this mismatch, the upper 16 bits were left unassigned, and having uninitialized signals into the mux blocked reading from the address locations sent by the apb\_mux\_tests
    *   The program would suspend during the ID\_Check call
    *   In the _cmsdk\_apb\_slave\_mux_ instantiation in **_cmsdk\_apb\_subsystem.v_**, I concatenated 0s to the 16-bit ssp\_prdata from the Ssp module
    ```scss
        .PSEL12            (ssp_psel),
        .PREADY12          (ssp_pready),
        .PRDATA12          ({16'h0000, ssp_prdata}),
        .PSLVERR12         (ssp_pslverr),
    ```
*   This then unblocked the test and the ID checks ran to completion
*   Since this test compares a predefined ID vector for each peripheral to verify the presence of the devices, additional peripherals being added are external to the test. An ID vector for the additional device must be added to the software test.
    *   I was able to obtain the IDs for the Ssp device by directly reading these values based on the address locations of the Ssp's data, these also matched the Peripheral Identification Register Value constants in **_SspDefs.v_** at the expected address locations (offset from 0x4000C000 which is the 4kB baseaddress allocated for APB expansion port 12)
    *   I added this vector as part of the test apb\_mux\_tests.c:
    ```cpp
    const unsigned char ssp_id[16]          = {0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00,
                                               0x22, 0x10, 0x34, 0x00, 0x0D, 0xF0, 0x05, 0xB1};
    ...
    puts ("12: APB expansion port 12 (SSp)");
        if ( ID_Check(&ssp_id[0],         0x4000C000          ) == 1 ) err_code |= 1<<12;
    ```

## Debug Tests (PASSED)
*   Running this test again after debugging the romtable test, seems like both rely on the cmsdk\_debug\_tester
*   With this new run, more of the test was able to run before hardfaulting, I will begin debugging from here

```yaml

```

*   Looks like the HardFault was caused by a similar issue in running the romtable test
*   Though the DEBUGTESTER macro should be able to read a valid address location at 0x0 from observing the debug\_tests.hex, it seems the program still stalls.

```cpp
// CM0 MCU's view of the memory shared with the debugtester
// (4 words above stack top)
// This macro uses the SP value from the vector table as stacktop
// The stacktop can not be set to the top of the memory.
#define DEBUGTESTERDATA ((volatile uint32_t *) *((uint32_t *) 0x0))
```

*   Instead of reading the MSP value at 0x0, this was replaced with the actual value of the MSP from the debug\_tests.hex program, which was 0x2000FC00 in `debug_test.h`

```cpp
// CM0 MCU's view of the memory shared with the debugtester
// (4 words above stack top)
// This macro uses the SP value from the vector table as stacktop
// The stacktop can not be set to the top of the memory.
#define DEBUGTESTERDATA ((volatile uint32_t *) 0x2000FC00)
```

## Dhry (PASSED)
*   Allowed test to run overnight to test assumption that the performance test takes a while to complete
*   Checked the status of this run the following morning but the simulation was still in the same state
*   Seems the issue has to do with printing strings
    *   I commented out the prints where the value was a string (i.e. char value\[31\]) and the test passed

    ```cpp
      // printf ("  Str_Comp:          %s\n", Ptr_Glob->variant.var_1.Str_Comp);
    ```

## DMA Tests (PASSED)

*   There may be other subsequent issues but for now, passing the APB Mux test is a prerequisite to running this test

*   Some updates concerning the **uDMA simple test:**
    *   Looking at the timing diagrams, the issue does not begin at the dma interfacing, but early on in the process of copying the contents of some source array into a destination array.
    *   In the test source code, a `source_data_array` and `dest_data_array` are initialized from 0 to 3 and 0 respectively.
    *   I've been able to trace this step in simulation by monitoring the writes to the SRAM at the address locations of the arrays.
    *   Below is a summary of the array locations:
    ```plain
    // Source Array
    [0] 200006ec
    [1] 200006f0
    [2] 200006f4
    [3] 200006f8
    // Destination Array
    [0] 200006fc
    [1] 20000700
    [2] 20000704
    [3] 20000708
    ```
*   Below is an image of the first stage of the dma transaction (blue arrows for dest\_array init and red for source array init)

![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/b60914e1-9583-48a4-87fe-4c1cc8c114b5/image.png)

*   There is a one cycle latency between writes to the SRAM and reads, so in this test the arrays are initially populated and written to the SRAM location as shown in the diagram above.
*   Then this data is sent to the ahb bus via the `ahb_slave_mux`.
    *   sram\_hrdata and sram\_hsel are inputs to this module, I'm expecting the same data to be available on the output of the mux before entering the ahb\_master\_mux and eventually dma
    *   For some reason, there is an issue with moving the data through the `ahb_slave_mux`
*   At first I thought the issue might be the additional pipelining stage in the synchronous SRAM module between read and writes, but I've tried removing/adding various pipelining stages, from the generation of sram\_hsel to the processing of sram\_hsel in the slave mux, and also modifying the behavioural SRAM model by removing the pipelining of reads, but this would break things
*   Since other transactions with the SRAM work as expected, I think the synchronous transaction between the SRAM and AHB bus is necessary.
*   I tried comparing the timing of one of these transactions between loading the SRAM and reading these values at the output of the `ahb_slave_mux`, and the timing behaviour does match what I see for the case of the sram transaction in the dma test, but the cycle offset where `sram_hsel` asserts and when the value of sram\_hrdata is available on the input of the mux differ by 1 cycle.
*   So then the wrong data is written to the `dmac_hrdata`, I'm not sure how to resolve this
*   According to the documentation, the transactions between the SRAM and AHB bus is behaving as expected, what I observe is similar to what the following timing diagram indicates:

![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/0fa5024a-6213-458e-8457-c905019838fe/image.png)
