###############################################################################
# N3: PS block level core signal verif (03-12-2025)
## **Link to important documentation:**
Presentation: [PS core signal verification.pptx](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/g/personal/sharanya_shetty_ixana_ai/ETLVYDYEqSNDorOyBVfIxFMBkSGCGoQZcKN3QKV9FLX5Dw?e=JWa7Jz)
includes the verification and code-coverage
### Where to run:
go to: /home/SharanyaShetty/digital/qs223/IC-RTL-qs223/vcs/power\_sequencer/verification\_core\_signal
enter the command: ./compile.rtl\_sim and ./simv\_rtl -gui &
## Timeline
**Task Progress Summary** [@Archisman Ghosh](#user_mention#96272410)
**03/12/2025:** Tested the core signal conditions

**Non-technical summary** [@Pushpal Das](#user_mention#89199382)
**3 Dec:** Completed verification of the core signal

**Note:**
// Parameters
parameter CLK\_FREQ\_MHZ = 32;
parameter CLK\_PERIOD = 1000.0 / CLK\_FREQ\_MHZ; // Clock period in ns
parameter DELAY\_CLK\_RESETN = 24'd320 - 2; // 10us at 32MHz (10us \* 32 cycles/us)
parameter DELAY\_START\_SIG = 24'd1600 - 2 - (24'd320); // 50us at 32MHz (50us \* 32 cycles/us)

tasks.name: N3: I2C testrun and debug

comments.text: Start date of Dec. 16
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

comments.text: Please mention the start date @Charles Adeyanju
comments.author: Pushpal Das

comments.text: @Pushpal Das the due date needs to be updated
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

comments.text: I will close this task, but create a separate task, for reunning by myself
comments.author: Archisman Ghosh

comments.text: @Pushpal Das Delayed Reason: Worked on this on and off as other higher priority tasks were raised
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

comments.text: @Archisman Ghosh Please review and close this task when you can
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

comments.text: Successfully added addressable status register and rx data register and was able to poll a busy flag while i2c transactions were ongoing to control storage of data buffer in user c code
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

comments.text: Also want to add a busy status flag that the user application can poll to wait while the i2c transaction is ongoing
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

comments.text: Working on making the internal reg_rx_buf addressable from the C user application, then I will move on to testing multi-byte reads
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

comments.text: I've been occupied with ssp slave testing and helping with the qs127m setup for firmware testing in the West Lafayette office
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

comments.text: Delayed reason @Pushpal Das: Have been working on other higher priority tasks like the 8-bit debug (86dypcqdh) as well as the FPGA debug issues (86dypcxqh). I'm currently still working on the Bypassing FPGA issue, so I will come back to this issue once those have been resolved as Shovan wants one of these boards working for QS223 validation by the firmware team.
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

comments.text:
Spent some time unblocking firmware with an issue with using datatypes that aren't uint32_t types, so this has been addressed now, that means we can use uint8_t, char, bool, uint16_t datatypes in applications.

I also worked on debugging another newly obtained faulty board. We'll need to get this returned to Avnet. I'll reference this in more detail in the Avnet tracking clickup task.
 I'm going to shift my focus back to i2c validation
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

comments.text: Delayed due to SSP debug
comments.author: Archisman Ghosh

comments.text: Working on some issues with connecting to core after integrating Ssp. Karan is facing some issues with the SPI bitstreams so I've temporarily paused work on this to unblock him. This is a very critical block as we need to test SPI post implementation
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

tasks.name: N3: Running M0 subsystem on FPGA (with Tucker)
tasks.description:
## 01/16/2026
*   Worked on debugging an issue with the VADJ rail not getting set, though the ZCU104 boards are supposed to come with VADJ factory programmed to 1.8V
*   Here are rough instructions for doing this
1. Download the zip folder `zcu104_restore_flash_1v8.zip` and unzip the folder to some location on your computer:

[zcu104\_restore\_flash\_1v8.zip](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/fdd0237b-d133-485d-b4dd-343143c40094/zcu104_restore_flash_1v8.zip)

2. Open **Vivado** and navigate to the hardware manager
3. In the tcl console, navigate to the unzipped folder, i.e. `cd C:/path/to/zcu104_restore_flash_1v8`
4. On the FPGA, set the dip switch (**SW6**) to JTAG mode if not already done
5. Power the FPGA and connect the FPGA and the PC over JTAG via a micro-usb cable
6. In the tcl console run: `source zcu104_program_qspi.tcl`
7. The above step takes about 7-10mins to erase the current contents of the QSPI flash and restores the first stage bootloader, this time the bootloader has been modified where Vadj has been set to 1.8V.
**IMPORTANT**: Programming the QSPI flash means the setting for Vadj does not persist across power cycles in JTAG mode. If a power cycle is done, you will need to run `source zcu104_program_qspi.tcl` (step 6) again. Vadj setting is only non-volatile when booting in QSPI mode.
## 01/20/2026
*   Tucker worked on porting the test source files for the tests that he deemed relevant to be tested on the FPGA
*   We worked together to run these ported CMSDK tests and the added tests created for external/custom peripherals
*   Here is a summary of the state of the tests (NOTE: I2C peripheral tests haven't been tried on the FPGA) according to Tucker:
    1. DMA
        1. **There is currently no status that tells what channel has triggered the interrupt (done or error)**
        2. exclude the test registers from the final ic?
        3. **Probably don't need 32 channels?**
        4. **DEBUG: when disconnecting after** `periph_dma` **it hardfaults?**
    2. GPIO
        1. really don't need the separated GPIO PORT0 handlers, they can be grouped in 2/4/8/... or just all together
        2. **the periph\_gpio test expects the GPIO0 pins to be pulled up, is that the case in the final IC? probably don't want that, pins are pushpull?**
        3. **there are no pullup resistors which is standard**
        4. **the ALT func pins only apply if a peripheral pin should be an output, this is not a huge problem but is a bit unconventional**
            1. **EX spi clk pin for Master is ALT=1 but for Slave is ALT=0**
    3. UART
        1. the rx in alt func has not effect, it should for it as an input though
        2. single uart rx/tx interrupt, there are already status flags too
    4. (Basic) Timer: **Not very useful**
        1. supports only freerunning
        2. no input div, so only counts at very high clock, requires potentially error prone (missing interrupts) sw management to count to larger values
        3. no single shot, so cannot be used for low-count single interrupts easily
        4. no pwm
    5. (Dual) Timer:
        1. **no pwm**
        2. **very limited dividers (1,16,256) which will limit precision or cause more involved SW control**
        3. **more timers, 2 is not enough to run most of our current apps**
    6. SPI Master
        1. **No DMA support**
        2. **There is only 1 SPI peripheral which will be used for the flash, no room for other peripheral**
        3. requires manual cs control
        4. Weird baud divider logic
        5. must manually discard RX if ignoring (such as during transmit or cmd portion)
        6. no direct support for cmd  r/w
            1. not through DMA either
        7. possible to reduce gap between words?
    7. SPI Slave
        1. **how does the GPIO ALT for SPI MISO not have an effect? should it not make it into an output if slave?**
        2. **the cs deassert between bytes is a hard requirement for Motorola which is not practical for real usage**
    8. UART
        1. the high-speed test mode can be removed? see the description below Table 4-7
    9. Watchdog
        1. Does not need such a high freq
*   Going to have a meeting with Archisman to go over these findings and what changes may need to be made in rtl and on the ASIC side.

tasks.description:
# List of Faulty FPGAs 1. [FPGA with Power Fault](https://app.clickup.com/t/86dy05kpb?block=block-8fe9b8d4-2736-457f-8f22-1a86ccfd4d5a) 1. **Status**: Received part replacement from Digikey ✅ 2. [FPGA with Clocking Fault](https://app.clickup.com/t/86dy05kpb?block=block-hJOvOJWk8B) 1. **Status**: Received refund from Avnet ✅ 3. [2 FPGAs with BRAM faults](https://app.clickup.com/t/86dy05kpb?block=block-an9PZJt6Op) 1. **Status**: Received 2x part replacements from Digikey ✅ * * * ### Avnet Email Thread ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/a1e5cb8c-599a-4ac5-a56d-0ee13251bf11/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/f7e37815-db4a-49c1-988e-c940745ef2b8/image.png) #### October 13, 2025: * Posted on Element14 Forum: [https://community.element14.com/technologies/fpga-group/f/forum/56273/issues-with-outputs-on-new-zcu104-eval-kit](https://community.element14.com/technologies/fpga-group/f/forum/56273/issues-with-outputs-on-new-zcu104-eval-kit) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/8fb9c822-41ee-4844-89a1-543fd5d0edd5/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/b5606bf0-9efd-47e7-93d2-2bd4a3d2392d/image.png) ### October 15, 2025 * Started RMA with AMD Support, case number: 00736544 ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/ad0526ec-0387-4436-a860-ff0dea58e72d/image.png) ### October 24, 2025 * Safa (New hire working with Riya) came in today, we discussed about my issues with the board * I stressed why it's not a problem with using the FPGA in the technical sense as various test bitstreams were flashed between the faulty FPGA and two identical FPGA boards, in particular issues with the on-chip clock * Safa got in touch with Avnet on the phone to relay these points and that we want the board returned * I've handed over all the parts and packaging that arrived last month to Safa to get ready to return * Looks like they'll be doing their own testing to determine if the board is indeed faulty # Safa Message Thread with Avnet ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/f4b42d39-9439-4575-b394-777a6aee9497/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/7aa1efff-e3f0-48d0-ab07-ebf1ac4221fb/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/af416d2c-61c5-4237-af66-1ee0a12e2ec7/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/8782d06c-4938-4319-bb91-85dba5069ac2/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/844efb2d-3f73-4bad-8a9d-7158e7f754bf/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/7e8be57b-8de7-408f-bafa-b6a458988d72/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/afe54876-f567-4f7e-8a3f-64e1becc43d4/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/3fbdfcc6-33dd-45e5-97d1-5840b555b59f/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/056b953c-7337-40dc-ae9f-68311f23e3c4/image.png) ### November 20, 2025 ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/6d2d0aea-d391-4097-b9b1-20ad709cc030/image.png) ### December 01, 2025 * New FPGA has failed due to some power issues. * Tried running its BIST (built-in self test) from QSPI flash, but none of the tests pass (test LEDs were off) * There is an LED (DS35) that indicates an accidental loss of power or an error in the PMU, this LED is red and I haven't been able to debug this. * Ran similar BIST for **first** failing FPGA and the outcome of the tests validate that it is a clocking issue based on the LED statuses (i.e., LED for clock test is OFF). ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/e25dca51-decf-46bb-96c2-c98188f9e076/image.png) * Top Left LED: Clocking and Memory test * Top Right LED: EEPROM and I2C test * Bottom Left LED: DIP Switch test * Bottom Right LED: Pushbutton test * We'll need to return both of these faulty boards ## December 3, 2025 * Apparently we need one of these boards to be up and running as soon as possible for 223 validation, so Shovan wanted me to look into some potential hack we can implement temporarily * However from more testing: ### FPGA with Power Fault (Case #44324955) * I tried flashing the **FSBL** (first stage bootloader program) on an SD card and booted the FPGA in this mode * Found that the PS\_INIT\_B LED was Red at times and Green * When it was green the system then attempts to run the BIST demo, testing the peripherals on the FPGA * But like previously indicated with the inconsistent PS\_INIT\_B LED, the BIST would suddenly halt * This behaviour was observed multiple times, where the BIST would run up to different points in its application based on different runs, it would never "freeze" at the same consistent point. So either it fails at startup indicated by the PS\_INIT\_LED or somewhere along the execution of the BIST. * The issue was replicated with the same **FSBL** in the board's qspi flash so it's not an issue with the application ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/5654c919-8554-4c2c-a3fd-98b64b41eeef/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/6dcb61ee-418f-41c2-9822-07a661333540/image.png) * The PS\_ERR\_OUT LED also turns red when attempting to restore the FPGA’s QSPI flash based on a support application provided by AMD. The following images above showcase the status of the LEDs in QSPI flash mode when running the BIST and in JTAG mode when attempting the restore flash program respectively.  * Additionally, I tried accessing the PMU directly (issues with PS\_ERR\_OUT LED indicates an issue with the PMU) using the Vitis XSDB server: ```perl XSDB Server URL: TCP:localhost:57065 xsdb% XSDB Server Channel: tcfchan#0 XSDB Started connect tcfchan#1 xsdb% connect tcfchan#1 xsdb% targets -set -filter {name =~ "xczu7_0"} no targets found with "name =~ "xczu7_0"". available targets: 1 PS TAP 2 PMU 3 PL 4 PSU (Cannot open JTAG port: AXI AP transaction error, DAP status 0x30000021) 5 RPU 6 Cortex-R5 #0 (Cannot open JTAG port) 7 Cortex-R5 #1 (Cannot open JTAG port) 8 APU 9 Cortex-A53 #0 (Cannot open JTAG port) 10 Cortex-A53 #1 (Cannot open JTAG port) 11 Cortex-A53 #2 (Cannot open JTAG port) 12 Cortex-A53 #3 (Cannot open JTAG port) xsdb% targets -set 2 xsdb% mrd 0xFFD80540 Memory read error at 0xFFD80540. MDM master access port not found xsdb% mrd 0xFFD80530 Memory read error at 0xFFD80530. MDM master access port not found xsdb% ``` * But I think the issue is something critical and unrecoverable as we can't even read the PMU's error registers over JTAG * **UPDATE:** Received email from DigiKey and this part is going to be replaced ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/81f4aa5f-5d4a-4c86-b739-37d42260e1fb/image.png) * Received email confirmation regarding the tracking number ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/64f4a252-9508-4aa6-8547-1d3d3474d357/image.png) [https://tools.usps.com/go/TrackConfirmAction\_input?qtc\_tLabels1=9205590106460363790935](https://tools.usps.com/go/TrackConfirmAction_input?qtc_tLabels1=9205590106460363790935) ### FPGA with Clocking Fault * Issue has to do with clocking * Other tests in the BIST and FSBL were completed successfully * Interestingly, the timer test passes but the clocking test fails, I'm not sure what the distinction between the two tests are since I don't have access to the source code. * This behaviour was observed after clearing the flash and reprogramming it with the FSBL image * Similar response when booting from an SD card with the same FSBL image * Because I don't see the PS\_INIT\_B issues and PS\_ERR\_OUT LED is off, this may be recoverable: ```markdown Xilinx Zynq MP First Stage Boot Loader Release 2018.3 Feb 27 2019 - 09:12:35 PMU-FW is not running, certain applications may not be supported. Clocking Test Failed Clocking test failed *********************************************************** *********************************************************** ** Zynq UltraScale+ MPSoC ZCU104 Evaluation Kit IPI Test ** *********************************************************** *********************************************************** Choose Feature to Test: 1: UART Test 2: LED Test 3: IIC Test 5: TIMER/SCUGIC Test 6: CAN Bus Test 7: SWITCH Test 8: PS DDR4 External Memory Test 9: DDR4 External Memory Test A: BRAM Internal Memory Test B: BUTTON Test C: Clocking Test D: PMOD Test G: System Monitor Test H: ETHERNET Loopback Test 0: Exit 1 ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - UART 00 Test ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** Testing UART 115200,8,N,1 Hello world! UART 00 Test Passed Press any key to return to main menu Choose Feature to Test: 1: UART Test 2: LED Test 3: IIC Test 5: TIMER/SCUGIC Test 6: CAN Bus Test 7: SWITCH Test 8: PS DDR4 External Memory Test 9: DDR4 External Memory Test A: BRAM Internal Memory Test B: BUTTON Test C: Clocking Test D: PMOD Test G: System Monitor Test H: ETHERNET Loopback Test 0: Exit 2 ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - GPIO LED Test ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** Watch the LEDs Press any key to return to main menu Choose Feature to Test: 1: UART Test 2: LED Test 3: IIC Test 5: TIMER/SCUGIC Test 6: CAN Bus Test 7: SWITCH Test 8: PS DDR4 External Memory Test 9: DDR4 External Memory Test A: BRAM Internal Memory Test B: BUTTON Test C: Clocking Test D: PMOD Test G: System Monitor Test H: ETHERNET Loopback Test 0: Exit 5 ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - SCU/GIC Test ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** Starting Scugic Self Test Example Scugic Self Test Example passed! ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - Timer Test ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** TTC Interrupt Example Test Successfully ran TTC Interrupt Example Test ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - Watchdog Timer Test ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** WDT SelfTest Example Test Successfully ran WDT SelfTest Example Test ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - Triple Timer Counter Test ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** Starting Timer Interrupt Example Timer Interrupt Example passed! Press any key to return to main menu Choose Feature to Test: 1: UART Test 2: LED Test 3: IIC Test 5: TIMER/SCUGIC Test 6: CAN Bus Test 7: SWITCH Test 8: PS DDR4 External Memory Test 9: DDR4 External Memory Test A: BRAM Internal Memory Test B: BUTTON Test C: Clocking Test D: PMOD Test G: System Monitor Test H: ETHERNET Loopback Test 0: Exit B ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - PL GPIO Pushbutton Test** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** Press top left button Press top right button Press bottom left button Press bottom right button Press any button Successfully ran PL GPIO Pushbutton Test Press any key to return to main menu Choose Feature to Test: 1: UART Test 2: LED Test 3: IIC Test 5: TIMER/SCUGIC Test 6: CAN Bus Test 7: SWITCH Test 8: PS DDR4 External Memory Test 9: DDR4 External Memory Test A: BRAM Internal Memory Test B: BUTTON Test C: Clocking Test D: PMOD Test G: System Monitor Test H: ETHERNET Loopback Test 0: Exit D ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - System Monitor Test ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** Starting System Monitor Example The Current Temperature is 38.969 Centigrades. The Maximum Temperature is 38.969 Centigrades. The Minimum Temperature is 38.908 Centigrades. The Current VCCINT is 0.850 Volts. The Maximum VCCINT is 0.850 Volts. The Minimum VCCINT is 0.849 Volts. Clock Frequencies ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** CLK_125 failed FMC_LPC_CLK0 failed FMC_LPC_CLK1 failed FMC_LPC_GBTCLK0 failed HDMI_DRU_CLOCK failed CLK_125 = 0.000 FMC_LPC_CLK0 = 0.000 FMC_LPC_CLK1 = 0.586 FMC_LPC_GBTCLK0 = 54.725 HDMI_DRU_CLOCK = 0.147 5 Clocks Failed Clocking Test Failed Press any key to return to main menu Choose Feature to Test: 1: UART Test 2: LED Test 3: IIC Test 5: TIMER/SCUGIC Test 6: CAN Bus Test 7: SWITCH Test 8: PS DDR4 External Memory Test 9: DDR4 External Memory Test A: BRAM Internal Memory Test B: BUTTON Test C: Clocking Test D: PMOD Test G: System Monitor Test H: ETHERNET Loopback Test 0: Exit C ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - Clock Frequencies ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** CLK_125 failed FMC_LPC_CLK0 failed FMC_LPC_CLK1 failed FMC_LPC_GBTCLK0 failed HDMI_DRU_CLOCK failed CLK_125 = 0.000 FMC_LPC_CLK0 = 0.513 FMC_LPC_CLK1 = 13.040 FMC_LPC_GBTCLK0 = 17.289 HDMI_DRU_CLOCK = 0.171 5 Clocks Failed Clocking Test Failed Press any key to return to main menu ``` * Here is the outcome of the clocking test in more detail: ```markdown ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - Clock Frequencies ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** CLK_125 failed FMC_LPC_CLK0 failed FMC_LPC_CLK1 failed FMC_LPC_GBTCLK0 failed HDMI_DRU_CLOCK failed CLK_125 = 0.000 FMC_LPC_CLK0 = 0.000 FMC_LPC_CLK1 = 0.586 FMC_LPC_GBTCLK0 = 54.725 HDMI_DRU_CLOCK = 0.147 5 Clocks Failed Clocking Test Failed ``` ### 2 FPGAs with BRAM faults (Case#: 44256933) My response to the DEFECTIVE QUESTIONS form from DigiKey Customer Resolution Team | Invoice Number | 119998848 | | ---| --- | | PO Number | | | Digi-Key Part Number | 122-2206-ND | | Internal Part Number | | | Quantity Affected |  2 | | Who should we contact for additional information?
(Please provide name, phone and/or email) | Charles Adeyanju
+1-765-543-9164
[charles.adeyanju@ixana.ai](mailto:charles.adeyanju@ixana.ai) | | Date/Lot Codes/Part Markings/Serial Number/Labels (if applicable please attach Pictures) | Serial Number #1: 895599062533-27882
Serial Number #2: 895599062533-27771 | | What is the failure rate? |  Both boards have failed | | Would you like to place a new order for replacements? |  Yes | 1. Does the part have externally visible physical damage? If yes, was the packaging damaged? 1. No sign of physical damage. Conducted a manufacture built-in self test and issue has to do with a fault in the BRAM resources 2. What is your desired outcome? (credit, replacement, evaluation) 1. Replacement 3. Please provide a detailed explanation of the issue/defect you are experiencing with the parts. 1. Fom running the BIST I see it's a BRAM issue. I was able to validate this by running the same BRAM test on the another FPGA that's in a good condition: **Passing test** ```markdown ******************************************************** ******************************************************** **                ZCU104 - PL BRAM TEST               ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** PL BRAM Memory Test Testing address range 0xB0000000-0xB00FFFFF. ERROR #1446: Address = 0xB0 ``` I've run steps like restoring the flash, power-cycling, and my LEDs indicate the power supply rails are good so I think it's a particular hardware fault. I did verify that the URAM resources are working fine, but with the nature of my design, I need BRAM resources. 1. Please provide a detailed explanation of your application. 1. The nature of my application is to return the two ZCU104 FPGA boards for replacement, this is within the 90-day warranty window as the boards were purchased on Jan 19, 2026. 2. At what point was the issue/defect noticed? (DOA, testing, field, etc.) 1. The defect was noticed while testing with the FPGAs * **UPDATE**: * Managed to get in touch with Digikey this afternoon over the phone, they concluded the 2 faulty BRAM FPGAs are manufacturer defects and they will be shipping replacements via FedEX. Since Avik is on file as the original buyer, they'll be sending him an automated email regarding the tracking information once the parts have been shipped. * I suspect we'll be getting the two replacements sometime next week as they offered an expedited shipping at no additional costs. Since these were manufacturer defects, we don't need to return the FPGAs either * Correction Order Number: 98166601 * Received the two FPGAs ## Conclusion * At this point we've received replacement FPGAs for all the faulty FPGAs * Because these are replacements, we still have the faulty FPGAs in the West Lafayette office * The FPGA with the power fault is unusable and essentially useless, but the two FPGAs with faulty BRAMs can be salvaged in future projects * There are many memory models available on the FPGA like URAMs, DRAM primitives that can be inferred on the FPGA if a design is not particularly constrained by a BRAM or needs a dual-port ram block * In general we have an abundance of FPGAs now in the West Lafayette office * 2 currently setup and in use * 2 ready to be used out of the box * 2 potentially usable with BRAM access issues

comments.text:
Avnet refunded the FPGA with the faulty clocks earlier this month on March 3rd. Attached is the refund receipt
9117092186 Refund Receipt.pdf
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

comments.text: Started another part replacement also from Digikey for the FPGA with the faulty power supply (previously suspected to be purchased from Avnet). There has been more communication activity with this case recently. I've explained things to them on a technical level and I'm awaiting the next steps from them
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

comments.text:
Contacted Digikey 2 weeks ago to start the process to replace 2 faulty FPGAs where their BRAMs have a permanent hardware fault. Submitted a defective part report and explained the issue on a more technical level and my debug steps to come to my conclusion. They mentioned they will be in touch on the next steps.

I've followed up with an email yesterday, but I will be calling them regarding updates on the case again today as it has been some time since I last got an update on this case.
comments.author: Charles Adeyanju

comments.text: @Pushpal Das still awaiting the next updates from Avnet

raw.name: Tracking Avnet/DigiKey reply and FPGA issues debug
raw.description:
# List of Faulty FPGAs 1. [FPGA with Power Fault](https://app.clickup.com/t/86dy05kpb?block=block-8fe9b8d4-2736-457f-8f22-1a86ccfd4d5a) 1. **Status**: Received part replacement from Digikey ✅ 2. [FPGA with Clocking Fault](https://app.clickup.com/t/86dy05kpb?block=block-hJOvOJWk8B) 1. **Status**: Received refund from Avnet ✅ 3. [2 FPGAs with BRAM faults](https://app.clickup.com/t/86dy05kpb?block=block-an9PZJt6Op) 1. **Status**: Received 2x part replacements from Digikey ✅ * * * ### Avnet Email Thread ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/a1e5cb8c-599a-4ac5-a56d-0ee13251bf11/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/f7e37815-db4a-49c1-988e-c940745ef2b8/image.png) #### October 13, 2025: * Posted on Element14 Forum: [https://community.element14.com/technologies/fpga-group/f/forum/56273/issues-with-outputs-on-new-zcu104-eval-kit](https://community.element14.com/technologies/fpga-group/f/forum/56273/issues-with-outputs-on-new-zcu104-eval-kit) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/8fb9c822-41ee-4844-89a1-543fd5d0edd5/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/b5606bf0-9efd-47e7-93d2-2bd4a3d2392d/image.png) ### October 15, 2025 * Started RMA with AMD Support, case number: 00736544 ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/ad0526ec-0387-4436-a860-ff0dea58e72d/image.png) ### October 24, 2025 * Safa (New hire working with Riya) came in today, we discussed about my issues with the board * I stressed why it's not a problem with using the FPGA in the technical sense as various test bitstreams were flashed between the faulty FPGA and two identical FPGA boards, in particular issues with the on-chip clock * Safa got in touch with Avnet on the phone to relay these points and that we want the board returned * I've handed over all the parts and packaging that arrived last month to Safa to get ready to return * Looks like they'll be doing their own testing to determine if the board is indeed faulty # Safa Message Thread with Avnet ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/f4b42d39-9439-4575-b394-777a6aee9497/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/7aa1efff-e3f0-48d0-ab07-ebf1ac4221fb/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/af416d2c-61c5-4237-af66-1ee0a12e2ec7/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/8782d06c-4938-4319-bb91-85dba5069ac2/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/844efb2d-3f73-4bad-8a9d-7158e7f754bf/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/7e8be57b-8de7-408f-bafa-b6a458988d72/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/afe54876-f567-4f7e-8a3f-64e1becc43d4/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/3fbdfcc6-33dd-45e5-97d1-5840b555b59f/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/056b953c-7337-40dc-ae9f-68311f23e3c4/image.png) ### November 20, 2025 ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/6d2d0aea-d391-4097-b9b1-20ad709cc030/image.png) ### December 01, 2025 * New FPGA has failed due to some power issues. * Tried running its BIST (built-in self test) from QSPI flash, but none of the tests pass (test LEDs were off) * There is an LED (DS35) that indicates an accidental loss of power or an error in the PMU, this LED is red and I haven't been able to debug this. * Ran similar BIST for **first** failing FPGA and the outcome of the tests validate that it is a clocking issue based on the LED statuses (i.e., LED for clock test is OFF). ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/e25dca51-decf-46bb-96c2-c98188f9e076/image.png) * Top Left LED: Clocking and Memory test * Top Right LED: EEPROM and I2C test * Bottom Left LED: DIP Switch test * Bottom Right LED: Pushbutton test * We'll need to return both of these faulty boards ## December 3, 2025 * Apparently we need one of these boards to be up and running as soon as possible for 223 validation, so Shovan wanted me to look into some potential hack we can implement temporarily * However from more testing: ### FPGA with Power Fault (Case #44324955) * I tried flashing the **FSBL** (first stage bootloader program) on an SD card and booted the FPGA in this mode * Found that the PS\_INIT\_B LED was Red at times and Green * When it was green the system then attempts to run the BIST demo, testing the peripherals on the FPGA * But like previously indicated with the inconsistent PS\_INIT\_B LED, the BIST would suddenly halt * This behaviour was observed multiple times, where the BIST would run up to different points in its application based on different runs, it would never "freeze" at the same consistent point. So either it fails at startup indicated by the PS\_INIT\_LED or somewhere along the execution of the BIST. * The issue was replicated with the same **FSBL** in the board's qspi flash so it's not an issue with the application ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/5654c919-8554-4c2c-a3fd-98b64b41eeef/image.png) ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/6dcb61ee-418f-41c2-9822-07a661333540/image.png) * The PS\_ERR\_OUT LED also turns red when attempting to restore the FPGA’s QSPI flash based on a support application provided by AMD. The following images above showcase the status of the LEDs in QSPI flash mode when running the BIST and in JTAG mode when attempting the restore flash program respectively.  * Additionally, I tried accessing the PMU directly (issues with PS\_ERR\_OUT LED indicates an issue with the PMU) using the Vitis XSDB server: ```perl XSDB Server URL: TCP:localhost:57065 xsdb% XSDB Server Channel: tcfchan#0 XSDB Started connect tcfchan#1 xsdb% connect tcfchan#1 xsdb% targets -set -filter {name =~ "xczu7_0"} no targets found with "name =~ "xczu7_0"". available targets: 1 PS TAP 2 PMU 3 PL 4 PSU (Cannot open JTAG port: AXI AP transaction error, DAP status 0x30000021) 5 RPU 6 Cortex-R5 #0 (Cannot open JTAG port) 7 Cortex-R5 #1 (Cannot open JTAG port) 8 APU 9 Cortex-A53 #0 (Cannot open JTAG port) 10 Cortex-A53 #1 (Cannot open JTAG port) 11 Cortex-A53 #2 (Cannot open JTAG port) 12 Cortex-A53 #3 (Cannot open JTAG port) xsdb% targets -set 2 xsdb% mrd 0xFFD80540 Memory read error at 0xFFD80540. MDM master access port not found xsdb% mrd 0xFFD80530 Memory read error at 0xFFD80530. MDM master access port not found xsdb% ``` * But I think the issue is something critical and unrecoverable as we can't even read the PMU's error registers over JTAG * **UPDATE:** Received email from DigiKey and this part is going to be replaced ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/81f4aa5f-5d4a-4c86-b739-37d42260e1fb/image.png) * Received email confirmation regarding the tracking number ![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/64f4a252-9508-4aa6-8547-1d3d3474d357/image.png) [https://tools.usps.com/go/TrackConfirmAction\_input?qtc\_tLabels1=9205590106460363790935](https://tools.usps.com/go/TrackConfirmAction_input?qtc_tLabels1=9205590106460363790935) ### FPGA with Clocking Fault * Issue has to do with clocking * Other tests in the BIST and FSBL were completed successfully * Interestingly, the timer test passes but the clocking test fails, I'm not sure what the distinction between the two tests are since I don't have access to the source code. * This behaviour was observed after clearing the flash and reprogramming it with the FSBL image * Similar response when booting from an SD card with the same FSBL image * Because I don't see the PS\_INIT\_B issues and PS\_ERR\_OUT LED is off, this may be recoverable: ```markdown Xilinx Zynq MP First Stage Boot Loader Release 2018.3 Feb 27 2019 - 09:12:35 PMU-FW is not running, certain applications may not be supported. UART 00 Test Passed Press any key to return to main menu Choose Feature to Test: 1: UART Test 2: LED Test 3: IIC Test 5: TIMER/SCUGIC Test 6: CAN Bus Test 7: SWITCH Test 8: PS DDR4 External Memory Test 9: DDR4 External Memory Test A: BRAM Internal Memory Test B: BUTTON Test C: Clocking Test D: PMOD Test G: System Monitor Test H: ETHERNET Loopback Test 0: Exit 2 ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - GPIO LED Test ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** Watch the LEDs Press any key to return to main menu Choose Feature to Test: 1: UART Test 2: LED Test 3: IIC Test 5: TIMER/SCUGIC Test 6: CAN Bus Test 7: SWITCH Test 8: PS DDR4 External Memory Test 9: DDR4 External Memory Test A: BRAM Internal Memory Test B: BUTTON Test C: Clocking Test D: PMOD Test G: System Monitor Test H: ETHERNET Loopback Test 0: Exit 5 ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - SCU/GIC Test ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** Starting Scugic Self Test Example Scugic Self Test Example passed! ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - Timer Test ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** TTC Interrupt Example Test Successfully ran TTC Interrupt Example Test ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - Watchdog Timer Test ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** WDT SelfTest Example Test Successfully ran WDT SelfTest Example Test ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - Triple Timer Counter Test ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** Starting Timer Interrupt Example Timer Interrupt Example passed! Press any key to return to main menu Choose Feature to Test: 1: UART Test 2: LED Test 3: IIC Test 5: TIMER/SCUGIC Test 6: CAN Bus Test 7: SWITCH Test 8: PS DDR4 External Memory Test 9: DDR4 External Memory Test A: BRAM Internal Memory Test B: BUTTON Test C: Clocking Test D: PMOD Test G: System Monitor Test H: ETHERNET Loopback Test 0: Exit B ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - PL GPIO Pushbutton Test** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** Press top left button Press top right button Press bottom left button Press bottom right button Press any button Successfully ran PL GPIO Pushbutton Test Press any key to return to main menu Choose Feature to Test: 1: UART Test 2: LED Test 3: IIC Test 5: TIMER/SCUGIC Test 6: CAN Bus Test 7: SWITCH Test 8: PS DDR4 External Memory Test 9: DDR4 External Memory Test A: BRAM Internal Memory Test B: BUTTON Test C: Clocking Test D: PMOD Test G: System Monitor Test H: ETHERNET Loopback Test 0: Exit D ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - System Monitor Test ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** Starting System Monitor Example The Current Temperature is 38.969 Centigrades. The Current VREFN is 0.000 Volts. System Monitor Example passed! Press any key to return to main menu Choose Feature to Test: 1: UART Test 2: LED Test 3: IIC Test 5: TIMER/SCUGIC Test 6: CAN Bus Test 7: SWITCH Test 8: PS DDR4 External Memory Test 9: DDR4 External Memory Test A: BRAM Internal Memory Test B: BUTTON Test C: Clocking Test D: PMOD Test G: System Monitor Test H: ETHERNET Loopback Test 0: Exit C ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - Clock Frequencies ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** CLK_125 failed FMC_LPC_CLK0 failed FMC_LPC_CLK1 failed FMC_LPC_GBTCLK0 failed HDMI_DRU_CLOCK failed CLK_125 = 0.000 FMC_LPC_CLK0 = 0.000 FMC_LPC_CLK1 = 0.586 FMC_LPC_GBTCLK0 = 54.725 HDMI_DRU_CLOCK = 0.147 5 Clocks Failed Clocking Test Failed Press any key to return to main menu Choose Feature to Test: 1: UART Test 2: LED Test 3: IIC Test 5: TIMER/SCUGIC Test 6: CAN Bus Test 7: SWITCH Test 8: PS DDR4 External Memory Test 9: DDR4 External Memory Test A: BRAM Internal Memory Test B: BUTTON Test C: Clocking Test D: PMOD Test G: System Monitor Test H: ETHERNET Loopback Test 0: Exit C ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - Clock Frequencies ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** CLK_125 failed FMC_LPC_CLK0 failed FMC_LPC_CLK1 failed FMC_LPC_GBTCLK0 failed HDMI_DRU_CLOCK failed CLK_125 = 0.000 FMC_LPC_CLK0 = 0.513 FMC_LPC_CLK1 = 13.040 FMC_LPC_GBTCLK0 = 17.289 HDMI_DRU_CLOCK = 0.171 5 Clocks Failed Clocking Test Failed Press any key to return to main menu ``` * Here is the outcome of the clocking test in more detail: ```markdown ******************************************************** ******************************************************** ** ZCU104 - Clock Frequencies ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** CLK_125 failed FMC_LPC_CLK0 failed FMC_LPC_CLK1 failed FMC_LPC_GBTCLK0 failed HDMI_DRU_CLOCK failed CLK_125 = 0.000 FMC_LPC_CLK0 = 0.000 FMC_LPC_CLK1 = 0.586 FMC_LPC_GBTCLK0 = 54.725 HDMI_DRU_CLOCK = 0.147 5 Clocks Failed Clocking Test Failed ``` ### 2 FPGAs with BRAM faults (Case#: 44256933) My response to the DEFECTIVE QUESTIONS form from DigiKey Customer Resolution Team | Invoice Number | 119998848 | | ---| --- | | PO Number | | | Digi-Key Part Number | 122-2206-ND | | Internal Part Number | | | Quantity Affected |  2 | | Who should we contact for additional information?
(Please provide name, phone and/or email) | Charles Adeyanju
+1-765-543-9164
[charles.adeyanju@ixana.ai](mailto:charles.adeyanju@ixana.ai) | | Date/Lot Codes/Part Markings/Serial Number/Labels (if applicable please attach Pictures) | Serial Number #1: 895599062533-27882
Serial Number #2: 895599062533-27771 | | What is the failure rate? |  Both boards have failed | | Would you like to place a new order for replacements? |  Yes | 1. Does the part have externally visible physical damage? If yes, was the packaging damaged? 1. No sign of physical damage. Conducted a manufacture built-in self test and issue has to do with a fault in the BRAM resources 2. What is your desired outcome? (credit, replacement, evaluation) 1. Replacement 3. Please provide a detailed explanation of the issue/defect you are experiencing with the parts. 1. Fom running the BIST I see it's a BRAM issue. I was able to validate this by running the same BRAM test on the another FPGA that's in a good condition: **Passing test** ```markdown ******************************************************** ******************************************************** **                ZCU104 - PL BRAM TEST               ** ******************************************************** ******************************************************** PL BRAM Memory Test Testing address range 0xB0000000-0xB00FFFFF. ERROR #1446: Address = 0xB0 ``` I've run steps like restoring the flash, power-cycling, and my LEDs indicate the power supply rails are good so I think it's a particular hardware fault. I did verify that the URAM resources are working fine, but with the nature of my design, I need BRAM resources. 1. Please provide a detailed explanation of your application. 1. The nature of my application is to return the two ZCU104 FPGA boards for replacement, this is within the 90-day warranty window as the boards were purchased on Jan 19, 2026. 2. At what point was the issue/defect noticed? (DOA, testing, field, etc.) 1. The defect was noticed while testing with the FPGAs * **UPDATE**: * Managed to get in touch with Digikey this afternoon over the phone, they concluded the 2 faulty BRAM FPGAs are manufacturer defects and they will be shipping replacements via FedEX. Since Avik is on file as the original buyer, they'll be sending him an automated email regarding the tracking information once the parts have been shipped. * I suspect we'll be getting the two replacements sometime next week as they offered an expedited shipping at no additional costs. Since these were manufacturer defects, we don't need to return the FPGAs either * Correction Order Number: 98166601 * Received the two FPGAs ## Conclusion * At this point we've received replacement FPGAs for all the faulty FPGAs * Because these are replacements, we still have the faulty FPGAs in the West Lafayette office * The FPGA with the power fault is unusable and essentially useless, but the two FPGAs with faulty BRAMs can be salvaged in future projects * There are many memory models available on the FPGA like URAMs, DRAM primitives that can be inferred on the FPGA if a design is not particularly constrained by a BRAM or needs a dual-port ram block * In general we have an abundance of FPGAs now in the West Lafayette office * 2 currently setup and in use * 2 ready to be used out of the box * 2 potentially usable with BRAM access issues
raw.creator: Archisman Ghosh

# Adiabatic TX – Status Update (Aug 21–22, 2025)
*   **Timeline:** Assigned on **Thu, Aug 21**; all deliverables completed by **Fri, Aug 22**.
*   **Python updates:**
    *   Refactored code to support **debug mode** and **deterministic file generation**.
    *   Produced clean hex outputs for I/Q and switch/index traces with consistent naming.
*   **Verification plan & wrapper:**
    *   Wrote a **test plan** and implemented the **test wrapper** accordingly.
    *   Wrapper sweeps OOK, carrier frequency, levels, cycle-reduction, and DR, with a gated checker on the DUT sampling clock and an initial sample skip for alignment.
*   **Vector generation:**
    *   **QPSK** and **BPSK** files generated and **uploaded to VNC** (not Git).
*   **KT & handoff:**
    *   Conducted **KT with Manav** on adiabatic verification; assigned him a **standalone testbench** for the special-case scenario.

**test plan:** [https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:x:/g/personal/sharanya\_shetty\_ixana\_ai/EebTFe3n971MiSnjSskeEOEBNOswBp481FAHxuvq7wshpQ?e=OP9iZh](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:x:/g/personal/sharanya_shetty_ixana_ai/EebTFe3n971MiSnjSskeEOEBNOswBp481FAHxuvq7wshpQ?e=OP9iZh)

comments.text:
Adiabatic testcase is working. Changes made:
Used 160M clk for adiabatic module instead of 100M
Increased the scale from 5 to 15 in Adiabatic_switches module to improve the signal power. The problem was happening because the signal power was very low and the filter truncation is distorting the signal because of which the packet itself was not being detected

How to run simulation & code coverage ?
1. Go to path : IC-RTL-qs223/vcs/qspi\_slave/verification\_script
2\. Run ./compile\_regression.sh on terminal
3\. Can check the simulation logs in Regression\_log folder
4\. Go to COVERAGE\_RESULTS folder
5\. Open terminal and run command for merged code coverage
urg $(find . -maxdepth 1 -type d -name "\*.vdb" -exec echo -dir {} \\;) -merge\_across\_libs -dbname qspi\_spi\_coverage.vdb
6\. Run command to view code coverage
verdi -cov -covdir qspi\_spi\_coverage.vdb

### **Task Progress Summary: SPI + QSPI Verification (Manav & Ushasi)**
### [@Archisman Ghosh](#user_mention#96272410)
**13/10/2025**
Knowledge transfer session with Ushasi on combined SPI and QSPI functionality.
Prepared a detailed verification test plan.
**14/10/2025**
Enhanced testbench: updated write/read tasks to support both **24-bit** and **16-bit** addresses.
Executed sanity checks to ensure correctness of basic transactions.
**15/10/2025**
Verified config memory and FIFO read/write operations.
Multiple debug rounds completed.
Integrated config\_mem\_controller\_top module replacing earlier wrapper.
Implemented status read test.
Implemented IRQ tests, initially failing and queued for debug.
Devised a set of **28 compile-time switches** based on RTL sync-up feedback to test various configurations.
**16/10/2025**
Debugged IRQ tests; RTL modifications were implemented to fix issues.
Successfully incorporated all **28 compile switches** into a single, scalable TB environment.
**17/10/2025**
Final debug round and regression runs executed successfully.
Achieved **96.07% code coverage** (without exclusions).
Coverage results added in slides for Sayantan’s review according to his bandwidth.

**The task was completed from our side on 17th October 2025 (Friday).**

### **Post-Completion Notes**
**23/10/2025**
Coverage review meeting with Sayantan held.
It was decided to mark redundant portions of RTL for exclusion and rerun regression + coverage.
**27/10/2025 (Planned)**
Follow-up review meeting for coverage exclusions.
Regression and coverage reruns will follow based on the review outcome.

* * *
### **Non-Technical Summary**
### [@Pushpal Das](#user_mention#89199382)
From 13th to 17th October, task was understood, the testbench was upgraded to handle QSPI+SPI together, multiple configurations were verified, high code coverage was achieved, and the task was completed successfully. Review was left.
* * *

comments.text:
1 - Task was given to me on 15/9/25 with due date as 18/9/25.

It took me 6 days to do the task.  Timeline is below.

 1 day  was used to understand the integration of config banks, device wrapper  (KT given by Sayantan)
1 day was used to implement those  in testbench and debug with help of Souptik
1 day was used to run agc ofc regressions 
2 days were used to run agc verififcation after making chnages in testbench (as this was previously done by her).

Waveforms review were done by @Archisman Ghosh on 25/9/25.Code coverage was done by 26/9/25. 

But then resetn_ofc & resetn_agc were given from configs so regressions  were done again and code coverage .

tasks.name: N3: ARM tool installation guide
tasks.description:
## Compiler Installation:
Documentation: [https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:f:/g/personal/archisman\_ghosh\_ixana\_ai/Er0kHVX62IhPoiYO2VPxVP0BdSH5NKS4CfdcL6Kf4eCq1A?e=qqvwlc](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:f:/g/personal/archisman_ghosh_ixana_ai/Er0kHVX62IhPoiYO2VPxVP0BdSH5NKS4CfdcL6Kf4eCq1A?e=qqvwlc) (reading is not necessary)
Note: Compiler user guide is the go-to guide for FW team.
### Installing the Compiler into the WSL environment
1. Download the compiler on your local PC from this link: [https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:u:/g/personal/archisman\_ghosh\_ixana\_ai/EXEd2jb8DdxNrxeaNdywe2EBv0PngCQdcqiFwt2PNEA2BA?e=huqMtE](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:u:/g/personal/archisman_ghosh_ixana_ai/EXEd2jb8DdxNrxeaNdywe2EBv0PngCQdcqiFwt2PNEA2BA?e=huqMtE)
    1. Please check 6.22.1 version is getting installed.
2. Copy the installed zip folder (Arm Compiler for Embedded FuSa 6.22.1 Toolchain and Qualification Kit (x86\_64 Linux) into the wsl environment
    1. This can be done graphically through the file explorer or using the copy command in windows:
        1. Navigate to repo containing the downloaded zip folder using the `cmd` terminal
        2. Run: `copy "Arm Compiler for Embedded FuSa 6.22.1 Toolchain and Qualification Kit (x86_64 Linux).zip" \\wsl.localhost\Ubuntu\home\<USER_NAME>`
    2. Alternatively, copy the downloaded zipped folder into the wsl `~`repository using Window File Explorer:
        1. Note: The wsl `~` repo is at: \\\\wsl.localhost\\Ubuntu\\home\\<USER\_NAME>
3. Unzip the downloaded zip folder
4. There will be two more zip folders:
    1. ARMCompiler6.22.1\_standalone\_linux-x86\_64
    2. Arm\_Compiler\_for\_Embedded\_FuSa\_Qualification\_Kit\_6.22.1\_linux
5. Unzip the ARMCompiler6.22.1\_standalone\_linux-x86\_64 folder
6. Navigate to unzipped repo and run the binary executable: `./install_x86_64.sh`
7. Follow the instructions in the terminal to install the compiler.
### Getting Started
After installation a folder will now be present in the home (~) repo
1. Navigate to /home/<USER\_NAME>/ArmCompilerforEmbeddedFuSa6.22.1/bin
2. In the bin repo, start a bash shell with the following command: `./suite_exec bash`
3. Run: `armlm inspect` and observe the following message: "No active licenses found in your local cache."
4. Talk to [@Archisman Ghosh](#user_mention#96272410) to obtain an activation code to the armlm server
5. To confirm successful license activation, run: `armlm activate --code xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxx`
    1. `armlm inspect` should show some similar output:
    ```yaml
    1 active product in your local cache:
    Hardware Success Kit (Standard)
        Product code: HWSKT-STD0
        Order Id: 0000229347
        License valid until: 2026-07-13
        Local cache expires in: 3 days and 2 hours
        Activation code: e11d42bb-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx
    ```
### Arm Licensing
[https://learn.arm.com/install-guides/license/cloud/](https://learn.arm.com/install-guides/license/cloud/)

[

developer.arm.com

https://developer.arm.com/support/licensing/user-based

](https://developer.arm.com/support/licensing/user-based)

### Possible Errors:
1. Network connectivity issues
    1. **Ongoing Case with ARM**:
    2. Fix according to [StackOverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/62314789/no-internet-connection-on-wsl-ubuntu-windows-subsystem-for-linux/63578387#63578387) issue, follow the steps outlined below: [https://stackoverflow.com/questions/62314789/no-internet-connection-on-wsl-ubuntu-windows-subsystem-for-linux/63578387#63578387](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/62314789/no-internet-connection-on-wsl-ubuntu-windows-subsystem-for-linux/63578387#63578387)
        1. Close the wsl terminal (Ubuntu)
        2. Open command prompt as an administrator and type these commands:
        ```plain
        netsh winsock reset 
        netsh int ip reset all
        netsh winhttp reset proxy
        ipconfig /flushdns
        ```
    3. Restart your PC
    4. After restarting, startup the wsl again and `ping google.com` to verify network connectivity
        ```python
        charles@Charles-PC:~$ ping google.com
        PING google.com (142.250.190.110) 56(84) bytes of data.
        64 bytes from ord37s35-in-f14.1e100.net (142.250.190.110): icmp_seq=1 ttl=117 time=6.52 ms
        64 bytes from ord37s35-in-f14.1e100.net (142.250.190.110): icmp_seq=2 ttl=117 time=8.51 ms
        64 bytes from ord37s35-in-f14.1e100.net (142.250.190.110): icmp_seq=3 ttl=117 time=7.38 ms
        64 bytes from ord37s35-in-f14.1e100.net (142.250.190.110): icmp_seq=4 ttl=117 time=7.11 ms
        64 bytes from ord37s35-in-f14.1e100.net (142.250.190.110): icmp_seq=5 ttl=117 time=7.46 ms
        ^C
        --- google.com ping statistics ---
        5 packets transmitted, 5 received, 0% packet loss, time 4130ms
        rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 6.521/7.396/8.505/0.644 ms
        ```
## Testing Compiler
1. Create a new repository and add a C program called main.c

```cpp
// Minimal program for ARM Cortex-M0: addition of two numbers
#include <stdio.h>

volatile int result; // Use volatile to prevent optimization

int main(void) {
    int a = 5;
    int b = 10;
    result = a + b;

    printf("Result: %d\n", result);

    while (1) {
        // Infinite loop to keep the program running
    }

    return 0; // This line will never be reache
}
```

2. To compile the program to an ELF file (i.e. main.elf) using the ARM compiler, in the project repo run: `../ArmCompilerforEmbeddedFuSa6.22.1/bin/armclang -mcpu=Cortex-M0 main.c -o main.elf --target=arm-arm-none-eabi`
3. The above command with generate a `main.elf` file in the same directory as `main.c`. This output is meant for ARM embedded targets, not for direct execution on the Linux machine.
4. Flash the `main.elf` or `main.bin` using a debugger/programmer to an ARM device and observe the output.
    1. To convert a `.elf` file to `.bin`, first check the elf format by running: `objdump -h main.elf | grep "file format"`
        1. An example output from the above command would be: `add.elf: file format elf32-little`
    2. Finally, run: `objcopy -I elf32-little -O binary main.elf main.bin`
## Compiling Tests in the Integration Kit
1. Make a repo for the demo using the following command: `mkdir demo`
2. You will need two files to be able to compile the prebuilt tests in the integration kit:
    1. [AT510-r0p0-03rel2-2.tar](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/personal/archisman_ghosh_ixana_ai/_layouts/15/onedrive.aspx?ga=1&startedResponseCatch=true&id=%2Fpersonal%2Farchisman%5Fghosh%5Fixana%5Fai%2FDocuments%2FWork%2FCPU%20exploration%2FARM%20Documents%2FAT510%2Dr0p0%2D03rel2%2D2%2Etar%2Egz&parent=%2Fpersonal%2Farchisman%5Fghosh%5Fixana%5Fai%2FDocuments%2FWork%2FCPU%20exploration%2FARM%20Documents)
    2. [CM0\_execution\_tb\_14Mar2024](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/personal/archisman_ghosh_ixana_ai/_layouts/15/onedrive.aspx?ga=1&startedResponseCatch=true&id=%2Fpersonal%2Farchisman%5Fghosh%5Fixana%5Fai%2FDocuments%2FWork%2FCPU%20exploration%2FARM%20Documents%2FCM0%5Fintegration%5Ftb%5Fpatch%2FCM0%5Fexecution%5Ftb%5F14Mar2024%2Etgz&parent=%2Fpersonal%2Farchisman%5Fghosh%5Fixana%5Fai%2FDocuments%2FWork%2FCPU%20exploration%2FARM%20Documents%2FCM0%5Fintegration%5Ftb%5Fpatch)
3. Copy the files into the wsl environment similar to the process to install the Arm compiler
4. Unzip the files
5. Copy the AT510-BU-50000-r0p0-03rel2 into the newly created `demo` folder
6. Navigate to the unzipped CM0\_execution\_tb folder, and copy the contests of this repo including the CMSIS folder: `CM0_execution_tb_14Mar2024/CM0_execution_tb/integration_kit/validation/tests` into the `/demo/AT510-BU-50000-r0p0-03rel2/integration_kit/validation/tests`
    1. Note: There will be popup dialogs to replace existing files, accept these dialogs. It is important to merge the two repos with these two processes, and not delete things manually
    2. Now, to compile the existing programs, i.e. hellow, run: `make hellow`.
        1. This will result in a hellow.elf and hellow.bin. These executables are meant for ARM embedded targets and can be verified in a simulation environment in the next section.
## Simulating Executables
1. Obtain/Setup a VNC server
    1. Note: Ensure you're added to the appropriate user group to have the right permissions
2. Configure SSH access with git, refer to this [Git Workflow Documentation](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:w:/g/personal/antony_paul_ixana_ai/ESTZ91QhaZVIroAltH59RD0BsfpyA4_IPf38jw8CNXKRbw?e=Xr70Xk) for more on using git and setting up SSH
3. Clone [IC-RTL-qs223](https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223) by running: `git clone git@github.com:Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223.git`
4. Navigate to the newly cloned repo, and this directory `/IC-RTL-qs223/vcs/ARM_M0/verification_vcs`
5. On the compiler machine (i.e. wsl environment), rename the output from previous section (i.e., `hellow.bin`, `main.bin`, etc.) to `image.bin` and download it onto the server machine
6. Move `image.bin` to `/IC-RTL-qs223/vcs/ARM_M0/verification_vcs`
    1. Note: You'll be prompted to replace the already existing image.bin. This is the executable that the simulation scripts reference.
7. To run the simulation, source the following scripts like so:
    1. `./compile.rtl_sim`
    2. `./simv_rtl`
8. This is the resulting output running hellow.bin in simulation:

![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/e24f60ba-fc5d-4251-b7ca-45831df19fac/image.png)

## Reference Files
#### Link for Integration Tests Summary:

[

t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com

https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/2f204d95-cfea-498f-b550-60acbe7ee68c/Arm%20Cortex%20M0%20Integration%20Tests.pdf?view=open

](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/2f204d95-cfea-498f-b550-60acbe7ee68c/Arm%20Cortex%20M0%20Integration%20Tests.pdf?view=open)

#### Integration kit Files:

[AT510-r0p0-03rel2-2.tar.gz](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/cb7e69d1-c5eb-4f75-bd00-a0821118c5cf/AT510-r0p0-03rel2-2.tar.gz)

[CM0\_execution\_tb\_14Mar2024.tgz](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/8c5be410-6766-4258-84d5-5eeae73feca6/CM0_execution_tb_14Mar2024.tgz)

comments.text: @Pushpal Das please consider 1st date done time. status was changed by mistake.
comments.author: Archisman Ghosh

4. Unzip the files
5. Copy the AT510-BU-50000-r0p0-03rel2 into the newly created `demo` folder
6. Navigate to the unzipped CM0\_execution\_tb folder, and copy the contests of this repo including the CMSIS folder: `CM0_execution_tb_14Mar2024/CM0_execution_tb/integration_kit/validation/tests` into the `/demo/AT510-BU-50000-r0p0-03rel2/integration_kit/validation/tests`
    1. Note: There will be popup dialogs to replace existing files, accept these dialogs. It is important to merge the two repos with these two processes, and not delete things manually
    2. Now, to compile the existing programs, i.e. hellow, run: `make hellow`.
        1. This will result in a hellow.elf and hellow.bin. These executables are meant for ARM embedded targets and can be verified in a simulation environment in the next section.
## Simulating Executables
1. Obtain/Setup a VNC server
    1. Note: Ensure you're added to the appropriate user group to have the right permissions
2. Configure SSH access with git, refer to this [Git Workflow Documentation](https://10xar-my.sharepoint.com/:w:/g/personal/antony_paul_ixana_ai/ESTZ91QhaZVIroAltH59RD0BsfpyA4_IPf38jw8CNXKRbw?e=Xr70Xk) for more on using git and setting up SSH
3. Clone [IC-RTL-qs223](https://github.com/Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223) by running: `git clone git@github.com:Quasistatics/IC-RTL-qs223.git`
4. Navigate to the newly cloned repo, and this directory `/IC-RTL-qs223/vcs/ARM_M0/verification_vcs`
5. On the compiler machine (i.e. wsl environment), rename the output from previous section (i.e., `hellow.bin`, `main.bin`, etc.) to `image.bin` and download it onto the server machine
6. Move `image.bin` to `/IC-RTL-qs223/vcs/ARM_M0/verification_vcs`
    1. Note: You'll be prompted to replace the already existing image.bin. This is the executable that the simulation scripts reference.
7. To run the simulation, source the following scripts like so:
    1. `./compile.rtl_sim`
    2. `./simv_rtl`
8. This is the resulting output running hellow.bin in simulation:

![](https://t9017196379.p.clickup-attachments.com/t9017196379/e24f60ba-fc5d-4251-b7ca-45831df19fac/image.png)

tasks.name: N3: FHSS Sync + Controller decision
tasks.description:
**Why?** FHSS Controller is important in the 223 IC for realizing the implementation of Narrow-Band FHSS scheme for UWB-based system.
**Deliverable:** a) Spec Document with RTL guideline b) Waveforms demonstrating functionality.
Risk:
Not finishing this task will create a blocker for system-level simulations and overall tapeout.

**Spec Document:**
FHSS Hop Specs ([https://app.clickup.com/9017196379/docs/8cqezuv-46917/8cqezuv-65057](https://app.clickup.com/9017196379/docs/8cqezuv-46917/8cqezuv-65057))

comments.text:
Timeline Summary – Delay in AES SDF Work
21–22 Aug (Thu–Fri): Focused on adiabatic testing as Manav was on leave.
25–26 Aug (Mon–Tue): Debugged adiabatic and RS cluster issues; all bugs resolved and separate meetings held.
27 Aug (Wed): On half-day leave for Ganesh Chaturthi. Before leaving, completed adiabatic verification and resolved RS testing bugs.
28 Aug (Thu) onward: Will resume pending tasks, including AES SDF.
Reason for Delay:
 Most of my bandwidth was consumed by adiabatic testing. I initially expected to assist Manav, but due to urgency, I had to take over the bulk of the work. Additionally, significant time went into trial runs for RS regression on clusters—being the first attempt, it surfaced new bugs that Antony hadn’t anticipated.
comments.author: Sharanya Shetty
